activation-function.alt: Schematic of one artificial neuron: inputs x1 through xn each scaled by a weight feed a summation node (the transfer function) that produces a net input, which then passes through an activation function box labeled phi to give the output, with a threshold input.
activation-function.caption: The activation function is the nonlinearity applied after a neuron sums its weighted inputs. Here the weighted sum (the transfer function) produces a net input, and the activation function phi maps it to the neuron's output. Without this nonlinear step a stack of layers would collapse into a single linear map, so the activation is what gives a network its expressive power.

adagrad.alt: Contour plot of an ill-conditioned objective with the AdaGrad optimizer path descending and turning to reach the minimum at the origin.
adagrad.caption: AdaGrad gives each parameter its own learning rate by dividing the step by the square root of the sum of that parameter's past squared gradients, so directions with large gradients are damped and rarely updated ones keep large steps. This per-parameter scaling lets it move efficiently across directions of very different steepness.
adagrad.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

ai-agent.alt: Block diagram of an agent interacting with its environment: percepts enter the AGENT box through Sensors, feed a What is the world like now block, then an Action to be done block driven by condition-action rules, and leave through Actuators as actions back into the ENVIRONMENT.
ai-agent.caption: An agent is a model placed in a loop with its environment: it takes in percepts through sensors, decides what to do, and emits actions through actuators, which change the environment and produce the next percepts. This perceive, decide, act cycle, repeated until the goal is met, is what separates an agent from a model that answers in a single pass.

approximate-nearest-neighbor.alt: A green query point at the center surrounded by red triangles and blue squares. A solid inner circle encloses the three points closest to the query, and a dashed outer circle encloses a larger set of nearby points.
approximate-nearest-neighbor.caption: Nearest neighbor search asks which stored points lie closest to a query (green). The inner circle captures its few nearest neighbors and the outer circle a wider set. Approximate methods find these neighbors without comparing against every point, trading a small, tunable chance of missing a true neighbor for an enormous gain in speed, which is what makes vector search practical at scale.

artificial-intelligence.alt: Three nested circles: a large outer circle labeled Artificial Intelligence, a smaller circle inside it labeled Machine Learning, and a smallest circle inside that labeled Deep Learning, each with a short definition.
artificial-intelligence.caption: Artificial intelligence is the broad outer container: any machine that mimics intelligent behavior. Machine learning is the subset that learns from data instead of hand-written rules, and deep learning is the narrower subset of machine learning that uses many-layered neural networks. The nesting shows that these are not rivals but successive answers to the same question.

attention-mechanism.alt: Overview diagram of an attention mechanism showing queries, keys, and values combined into weighted outputs
attention-mechanism.caption: Attention scores every key against a query, then returns a weighted blend of the values, letting the model focus on the inputs that matter most.
attention-mechanism.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

autoencoder.alt: Schematic of an autoencoder showing an encoder compressing input to a latent code and a decoder reconstructing the output
autoencoder.caption: An autoencoder compresses an input to a small latent code and reconstructs it; the bottleneck forces the network to keep only the most informative structure.

automatic-differentiation.alt: A computation graph for a function of two inputs built bottom to top through operator nodes (sin, times, plus); a large arrow down the left marks the backward propagation of derivative values, with adjoint quantities flowing from the output (seeded at one) back to each input.
automatic-differentiation.caption: Automatic differentiation records a program as a graph of elementary operations and applies the chain rule across it to get exact derivatives. In reverse mode, shown here, a forward pass computes the values and a single backward pass propagates derivative contributions from the output (seeded at one) back to every input; applied to a neural network this reverse pass is exactly backpropagation.

automatic-speech-recognition.alt: The architecture of OpenAI's Whisper speech model. On the left, examples of its multitask training data: English transcription, speech translation, non-English transcription, and no-speech audio. In the center, audio becomes a log-mel spectrogram, passes through convolution layers into a transformer encoder, and a transformer decoder with cross-attention emits text tokens.
automatic-speech-recognition.caption: Modern automatic speech recognition is end-to-end. The audio is turned into a log-mel spectrogram, a transformer encoder reads it, and a transformer decoder generates the text one token at a time, the same encoder-decoder setup used for translation. Whisper, shown here, is trained on a large and varied corpus of audio paired with transcripts, which is what makes it robust across languages, accents, and noise.
automatic-speech-recognition.credit: Alec Radford, Jong Wook Kim, Tao Xu, Greg Brockman, Christine McLeavey, Ilya Sutskever

backpropagation.alt: Computational graph of a small one-hidden-layer network: the input x is multiplied by weights to give z, an activation gives h, a second weight multiply gives the output o, which with the labels yields the loss; weight-decay terms feed a regularization term s, and the loss plus s give the objective J.
backpropagation.caption: Backpropagation treats the network as a computational graph of operations and intermediate values, like this forward graph from input x through the hidden activation h to the loss and objective J. It then applies the chain rule in reverse along these same edges, computing the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight in a single backward pass that costs about as much as the forward one.
backpropagation.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

bag-of-words.alt: On the left, a Renaissance portrait of a woman. On the right, the same portrait cut into small fragments (an eye, the mouth, the necklace, a patch of hair) scattered loosely inside an open paper bag.
bag-of-words.caption: The bag-of-words model represents a text by which pieces it contains while discarding their order, like cutting a portrait into fragments and dropping them into a bag. The collection of parts is kept but the arrangement is lost, so 'the dog bit the man' and 'the man bit the dog' look identical. For many tasks the words present matter more than their order, which is why this crude representation works.

basis.alt: A 2D grid with two unit arrows from the origin: e1 pointing one unit along the horizontal axis and e2 pointing one unit along the vertical axis.
basis.caption: A basis is a minimal set of independent directions from which every vector in the space is built uniquely by scaling and adding. The standard basis e1 and e2 spans the plane, so any vector is just so much e1 plus so much e2, and those amounts are exactly its coordinates.

batch-size.alt: Plot of training loss versus wall-clock time on a log scale, comparing full-batch gradient descent, single-example stochastic gradient descent, and mini-batches of size 100 and 10.
batch-size.caption: Batch size is how many examples are used per update, and it trades gradient quality against speed. Full-batch descent takes accurate but slow steps and single-example updates are noisy; moderate mini-batches reach a low loss fastest in wall-clock time by using hardware efficiently while keeping the gradient stable.
batch-size.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

bayes-theorem.alt: Two probability tree diagrams of the same two events: the top tree branches on A first then B, the bottom tree branches on B first then A, with Bayes' theorem written between them.
bayes-theorem.caption: Bayes' theorem inverts the direction of conditioning. The same joint probabilities can be factored two ways, conditioning on A first or on B first; Bayes' rule is the bridge that converts P(B given A) into P(A given B).

bayesian-inference.alt: Three panels labeled Prior, Likelihood, and Posterior: a broad prior over a parameter, a likelihood from the data, and a narrower posterior concentrated where the two agree.
bayesian-inference.caption: Bayesian inference combines a prior distribution of belief with the likelihood of the observed data to produce a posterior distribution. The posterior concentrates where prior and data agree, carrying uncertainty forward rather than returning a single point estimate.

bayesian-optimization.alt: Top panel: a black true-objective curve with several evaluated points, overlaid by a Gaussian process surrogate drawn as a mean curve inside a purple uncertainty band that is wide where there is no data. Three lower panels show acquisition functions, expected improvement, upper confidence bound, and probability of improvement, each marking a next point to sample with a gold diamond.
bayesian-optimization.caption: Bayesian optimization fits a probabilistic surrogate (the purple Gaussian process band) to the points evaluated so far, predicting both an expected value and a calibrated uncertainty that is wide where the space is unexplored. An acquisition function (lower panels) turns that belief into a score, balancing sampling where the value looks good against sampling where uncertainty is high, and its peak (gold diamond) chooses the single most informative point to evaluate next.

beam-search.alt: A branching diagram across three time steps with beam width two. From the start, candidates A through E are expanded but only A and C are kept (highlighted); each is expanded again, keeping AB and CE; then again, reaching ABD and CED.
beam-search.caption: Beam search keeps the few best partial sequences at every step rather than just one. With a beam width of two, all next tokens are expanded but only the two highest-scoring sequences survive each step (A and C, then AB and CE, then ABD and CED). Carrying several hypotheses forward lets a locally weaker token prove it leads somewhere better, which greedy decoding (beam width one) cannot do.
beam-search.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

bert.alt: A diagram of the BERT masked-language-modeling task. A row of input tokens for a sentence has one token replaced by [MASK]; all tokens pass up into a box labeled 'BERT = Transformer Encoder', and a feed-forward head over the masked position outputs logits predicting the hidden word.
bert.caption: BERT is a stack of transformer encoder layers pretrained by masked language modeling: a token in the input is replaced by [MASK], and the model must recover it using the words on both sides at once. Because every position can attend to every other, BERT produces deeply bidirectional, context-sensitive representations, so the same word gets different vectors in different sentences.

bi-encoder.alt: A symmetric two-tower diagram. Sentence A and Sentence B each pass up through their own BERT block, then a pooling step, producing vectors u and v. The two vectors feed a single cosine-sim(u, v) box at the top, whose output ranges from minus 1 to 1.
bi-encoder.caption: A bi-encoder runs each text down its own tower, encoding the query and the document independently into vectors u and v, then scores relevance by a cheap similarity such as cosine. Because a document's vector never depends on the query, every document can be embedded once and indexed ahead of time, so search becomes a fast nearest-neighbor lookup. That independence is what lets a bi-encoder scale to a large corpus.

bias-variance-tradeoff.alt: Plot of error against model complexity showing bias squared falling as complexity rises, variance rising, and the total error forming a U-shaped curve with a marked optimum at the bottom.
bias-variance-tradeoff.caption: As model complexity grows, bias falls but variance rises, and their sum, the total error, traces a U. The lowest point marks the optimal complexity: simpler models underfit on the left with high bias, more complex models overfit on the right with high variance, and no single model drives both errors to zero at once.

calibration.alt: A reliability diagram plotting the observed fraction of positives against the mean predicted probability for several classifiers, with a dotted diagonal labeled perfectly calibrated, and a histogram below showing how the predictions are distributed.
calibration.caption: A reliability diagram bins predictions by their stated probability and plots, for each bin, how often those predictions were actually correct. A perfectly calibrated model lies on the diagonal; curves below it (here support vector classification) are overconfident, and curves above it are underconfident.

catastrophic-forgetting.alt: Three line plots tracking a model's score on many held-out benchmarks across successive stages of continual instruction tuning, labeled from Initial through later tuning stages. Almost every line falls from its initial value as more tuning stages are applied.
catastrophic-forgetting.caption: Catastrophic forgetting is visible here as a steady decline: as a model is fine-tuned on a sequence of new tasks (left to right), its measured ability on previously learned benchmarks drops, because the shared weights that stored the old skills are overwritten to fit the new data. Nothing in the loss on current data protects the earlier knowledge, so the degradation can be broad and severe.
catastrophic-forgetting.credit: Yun Luo, Zhen Yang, Fandong Meng, Yafu Li, Jie Zhou, Yue Zhang

category-theory.alt: On the left, a vertical arrow f from object X to object Y. On the right, a square whose four corners are F(X), G(X), F(Y), G(Y); the vertical arrows are F(f) and G(f), and the horizontal arrows are the components eta-X and eta-Y.
category-theory.caption: The three tiers of category theory in one picture. A morphism f between objects X and Y (left) is sent by two functors F and G to the vertical maps F(f) and G(f), and a natural transformation supplies the horizontal components eta-X and eta-Y. The square commuting (both paths from F(X) to G(Y) agree) is exactly what makes the transformation natural: it relates the two constructions uniformly across every object.

causal-attention.alt: Block diagram of scaled dot-product attention flowing upward: queries and keys enter a matrix multiply, then a scale step, then a mask step, then softmax to produce attention scores, which a final matrix multiply combines with the values to give the context vector.
causal-attention.caption: Causal attention is scaled dot-product attention with the mask step shown here doing the work. After the query-key scores are scaled, the mask sets every score that points to a future token to negative infinity, so the following softmax gives those positions zero weight. Each position can then attend only to itself and earlier tokens, which is what lets a model be trained on next-token prediction over a whole sequence at once.

central-limit-theorem.alt: Panels showing the distribution of the sum of n dice for n from 1 to 5: a flat bar chart for one die that grows more bell-shaped as more dice are added, with a final panel overlaying the curves against a normal.
central-limit-theorem.caption: The central limit theorem says sums of many independent variables tend toward a Gaussian. A single die is flat and uniform, but the distribution of the sum of several dice grows into the familiar bell curve as their number increases.

chain-of-thought.alt: Two side-by-side prompt examples. Under Standard Prompting the model is given a worked example then answers a new question directly and gets it wrong (red). Under Chain-of-Thought Prompting the example includes step-by-step reasoning (highlighted), and the model writes out its own reasoning before giving the correct answer (green).
chain-of-thought.caption: Chain-of-thought prompting has the model write out intermediate reasoning steps (highlighted) before its final answer, rather than jumping straight to it. On multi-step problems this raises accuracy: the direct answer on the left is wrong, while the worked reasoning on the right reaches the right one, because each generated step is extra computation the later steps can build on.
chain-of-thought.credit: Jason Wei, Xuezhi Wang, Dale Schuurmans, Maarten Bosma, Brian Ichter, Fei Xia, Ed Chi, Quoc Le, Denny Zhou

chain-rule.alt: A computation graph for a function of two inputs, with square input nodes feeding circular operator nodes (sin, times, plus) up to the output; each edge is annotated with a derivative formula that multiplies and sums contributions along the paths.
chain-rule.caption: The chain rule differentiates a composition by multiplying the local rates of change along the chain, summing over every path from an input to the output. Here the derivative of each node is built from the derivatives of the nodes feeding it (for example the sin node contributes cos(w1) times the incoming rate), which is exactly how a network's gradients are assembled.

china-brain.alt: The outline of a human brain drawn entirely out of many small identical figures of people, each standing for one person in the population.
china-brain.caption: The China Brain asks us to imagine every person in a huge population acting as a single neuron, passing signals by radio so the whole nation reproduces the functional organization of a brain. If functionalism is true the network should have the brain's mental states, yet it seems incredible that a billion people exchanging messages add up to one conscious subject, which is the absent-qualia objection the image makes vivid.

circuit.alt: Three feature detectors on the left, labeled Windows, Car Body, and Wheels, each shown as an optimized image next to a grid of its spatial weights colored red for positive (excitation) and blue for negative (inhibition); arrows feed them into a car detector on the right, captioned as assembled from these earlier units.
circuit.caption: A circuit is a set of named feature detectors wired together by weights to compute a higher-level feature. Here window, car-body, and wheel detectors combine into a car detector: the weight maps show that windows excite the car detector at the top and inhibit it at the bottom, while wheels do the reverse, so the spatial arrangement of parts, not just their presence, is what the circuit checks.
circuit.credit: Olah, Cammarata, Schubert, Goh, Petrov, Carter

classification.alt: A grid comparing the decision boundaries of many classifiers across three two-dimensional datasets. Each cell shades the plane into red and blue regions for the two classes, with the training and test points overlaid, and the boundaries differ in shape from one classifier to the next.
classification.caption: Classification assigns each input to one of a fixed set of categories by carving the feature space into regions, one per class. Running several classifiers on the same data shows that each draws the boundary differently: some are straight, others curved or patchy. The shaded regions are the learned decision rule, and a new point is labeled by the region it lands in.

clustering.alt: A grid of scatter plots: rows are different shaped datasets (two concentric circles, two interleaving moons, blobs, anisotropic and varied-variance blobs, and uniform noise) and columns are different clustering algorithms, each coloring the points by the cluster it assigned.
clustering.caption: Clustering groups points by similarity with no labels, but algorithms disagree about what a cluster is. Across these dataset shapes, center-based methods like k-means split the concentric circles and moons wrongly, while density-based methods recover the curved shapes. There is no single correct clustering, because the chosen notion of similarity quietly decides the answer.

collective-communication.alt: Four panels showing ring synchronization across four GPUs arranged in a ring. In each successive panel a GPU sends a segment of its gradient vector to its left neighbor, which adds it in, so the accumulated segments march around the ring until every device holds the fully summed result.
collective-communication.caption: Collective operations synchronize tensors across a whole group of devices at once. This is the ring algorithm for an all-reduce: each GPU is arranged in a ring and repeatedly passes one slice of its gradient to a neighbor, which accumulates it, until every device ends up with the complete sum. Casting the reduction as a ring keeps each link busy and makes the cost almost independent of the number of GPUs.
collective-communication.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

computational-theory-of-mind.alt: A diagram of a Turing machine: a tape divided into cells holding the symbols a, b, a and blanks, a read/write head pointing at one cell, and a Finite Control box below listing states q0, q1, q2, and h with an arrow indicating a transition.
computational-theory-of-mind.caption: The computational theory of mind holds that thinking just is computation of this kind. A Turing machine has a tape of symbols (the representations), a read/write head, and a finite control that rewrites symbols by formal rules (the program). The claim is that mental states are representations and thought is the rule-governed manipulation of them, so a mind could in principle run on any hardware that carries out the right computation.

concentration-inequality.alt: A plot of the probability that a sum of n random variables deviates from its mean by at least x times the square root of n. Two decreasing curves are shown: a red Hoeffding bound that drops off very fast, and a blue Chebyshev bound that drops off more slowly, with the red curve below the blue one for larger deviations.
concentration-inequality.caption: A concentration inequality bounds the chance that an average strays far from its mean. Plotted against the size of the deviation, the Hoeffding bound (red) decays exponentially and is far tighter than the older Chebyshev bound (blue), which decays only polynomially. This exponential control of large deviations is the engine beneath every generalization guarantee in learning theory.

conditional-probability.alt: Venn diagram of the numbers 1 to 10 split into a prime set and an even set, with only the number 2 in the overlap of both.
conditional-probability.caption: Conditioning restricts attention to a smaller world and renormalizes. Among 1 to 10 the even numbers are 2, 4, 6, 8, 10; given that a draw is even, the chance it is also prime is just one in five, since 2 is the only even prime.

conditional-random-field.alt: A trellis grid with five columns for time steps t=1 to t=5 and five rows for possible states i=1 to i=5, every cell a circle. Most circles are gray, and one circle per column is ringed and joined by a bold line into a single path that zigzags across the columns.
conditional-random-field.caption: Sequence labeling can be drawn as a trellis: at each position the token could take any of several labels (the rows), and the bold path is the one assignment chosen for the whole sequence. A conditional random field scores entire label paths rather than each position alone, using the transitions between neighboring labels, and selects the single best consistent path through the lattice.

confusion-matrix.alt: Four copies of a two-by-two confusion matrix with predicted class on the columns and real class on the rows, each highlighting a different metric computed from the true positive, true negative, false positive, and false negative cells: accuracy, precision, recall, and specificity.
confusion-matrix.caption: A confusion matrix tabulates predictions against truth, splitting them into true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives. Almost every classification metric is a ratio of these four cells: accuracy uses all four, precision divides true positives by all predicted positives, and recall divides them by all actual positives. The breakdown reveals which kind of error a model makes, not just how often it is wrong.

conjugate-gradient.alt: Contour plot of an elongated quadratic bowl with two paths from a start point x0 to the minimum x: a red steepest-descent path that zig-zags down the valley and a green conjugate-gradient path that reaches the minimum in two straight steps.
conjugate-gradient.caption: On a stretched quadratic bowl, steepest descent (red) steps perpendicular to the contours and slowly zig-zags down the valley, undoing part of its own progress each step. Conjugate gradient (green) instead picks directions that do not interfere with each other, so for a quadratic in two variables it reaches the exact minimum in just two steps.

connectionism.alt: A row of five units, some filled dark and some empty, each labeled with a feature (singular, masculine, first, second, third person), so that a single concept is encoded as the pattern across all five units.
connectionism.caption: Connectionism stores knowledge in the pattern of activity across many simple units rather than in any one place. A concept is a distributed code: here it is the particular combination of which feature units are on (dark) and which are off, so meaning lives in the whole pattern, not a single dedicated unit.

constitutional-ai.alt: A two-phase flow. In the supervised phase, a helpful model generates responses to red-teaming prompts, then critiques and revises each response against a principle, and the revised responses fine-tune a supervised model. In the reinforcement phase, the model compares pairs of its own responses to produce AI preference labels, which train a preference model used to optimize the final model.
constitutional-ai.caption: Constitutional AI aligns a model using AI feedback guided by a written set of principles. In the supervised phase the model critiques and revises its own answers against a principle, and the revisions become fine-tuning data. In the reinforcement phase the model ranks pairs of its own responses by how well they satisfy the constitution, and those AI-generated preferences train a preference model, shifting most of the labeling from humans to the model.

contrastive-decoding.alt: An expert language model and a smaller amateur model each give next-token probabilities for a prompt about where Barack Obama was born. The contrastive score is the expert log-probability minus the amateur log-probability: 1961 scores highest at 4.13, Hawaii 2.34, Washington minus 0.73. Sample continuations show greedy repeating Hawaii, nucleus drifting to Washington, and contrastive decoding correctly continuing with 1961.
contrastive-decoding.caption: Contrastive decoding scores each token by the gap between a strong expert model and a weak amateur model's log-probabilities, preferring what the expert uniquely favors. Generic or repetitive tokens that even the amateur likes (Hawaii) are cancelled out, so the method avoids the greedy loop and the nucleus drift and continues with the distinctive correct token (1961).

contrastive-learning.alt: Two circles labeled before and after learning. In the left circle an anchor, a green positive, and a red negative sit at varied distances. An arrow labeled learning leads to the right circle, where the positive has moved close to the anchor and the negative has moved far away.
contrastive-learning.caption: Contrastive learning shapes a vector space by comparison. For each anchor it pulls a matching example (the positive, green) closer and pushes a non-matching one (the negative, red) farther apart, as the before-and-after shows. Repeated over many such triples, this teaches the single rule that distance should mean dissimilarity, which is why the resulting embeddings power dense retrieval and semantic search.

convex-optimization.alt: Two panels. The top, labeled Not convex, is a wavy curve where the straight segment joining two points on it dips below the curve. The bottom, labeled Convex, is a single bowl where the segment joining two points stays above the curve.
convex-optimization.caption: A function is convex when the straight line between any two points on its graph lies on or above the curve (bottom). This guarantees a single valley, so any local minimum is the global minimum, which is why convex problems can be solved to provable optimality. A non-convex function (top) has multiple dips where an optimizer can get trapped.

convexity.alt: An upward-curving red function with a straight chord drawn between two of its points, P and Q; the chord lies entirely on or above the curve.
convexity.caption: A function is convex when the straight segment joining any two points on its graph lies on or above the graph itself, as the chord from P to Q does here. The curve never dips below its own chords, which is why a convex function curves upward everywhere and has a single lowest region rather than many separate dips.

convolutional-neural-network.alt: Pipeline of a typical convolutional neural network: an input image, then alternating convolution and subsampling stages that produce progressively smaller stacks of feature maps, ending in a fully connected layer and an output.
convolutional-neural-network.caption: A convolutional neural network alternates convolution stages, which slide learnable filters over the input to build feature maps, with subsampling stages that shrink those maps. Early layers capture low-level patterns and deeper layers compose them into objects, before a fully connected layer maps the features to an output.

coordinate-descent.alt: Contour plot of the quadratic function f(x,y) equals 5x squared minus 6xy plus 5y squared, with a red path that moves to the minimum in alternating axis-aligned segments: first a vertical move, then a horizontal move, then vertical again, each step shrinking toward the center.
coordinate-descent.caption: Coordinate descent minimizes along one axis at a time. Each red segment holds every variable fixed but one and finds the best value along that single direction, so the path moves in alternating vertical and horizontal steps. This turns a hard multidimensional problem into a sequence of easy one-dimensional minimizations that spiral in toward the minimum.

covariance.alt: Grid of scatter plots: a top row showing correlation coefficients from plus one through zero to minus one, and a bottom row of strongly patterned shapes that all have a correlation of zero.
covariance.caption: Covariance, normalized into the correlation coefficient, measures how two variables move together, from plus one through zero to minus one (top rows). It captures only linear association: the strongly dependent shapes in the bottom row all have a correlation of zero.

cross-attention.alt: A decoder block. The target sequence enters masked multi-headed self-attention at the bottom, supplying its query, key, and value. Its output supplies the query to a multi-headed cross-attention block above, while the key and value for that block come from the encoder's states arriving from the left. A feed-forward network and the prediction sit on top.
cross-attention.caption: Cross-attention is the middle block here, and the labels show its defining feature: the query (Q) comes from the decoder's own sequence below, while the key (K) and value (V) come from the encoder's states on the left. Drawing queries from one sequence and keys and values from another is what lets a model condition its output on a separate input, such as a source sentence to translate.

cross-encoder.alt: Two architectures side by side. On the left, the Bi-Encoder sends Sentence A and Sentence B through separate BERT and pooling towers into vectors u and v, joined by a Cosine-Similarity box. On the right, the Cross-Encoder feeds both sentences together into one BERT and a Classifier that outputs a single score from 0 to 1.
cross-encoder.caption: A cross-encoder (right) feeds the query and a candidate document into one model together, so attention runs across both texts before it outputs a single relevance score. That joint reading is more accurate than the bi-encoder (left), which encodes each text separately and compares finished vectors. The cost is that nothing can be precomputed, so the cross-encoder runs once per pair and is used to rerank a shortlist rather than scan a whole corpus.

cross-entropy.alt: Curve of log loss against the probability the model assigns to the true class, near zero when that probability approaches one and rising steeply toward infinity as it approaches zero.
cross-entropy.caption: Cross-entropy, the log loss used to train classifiers, penalizes the probability a model assigns to the correct class. The loss is small when that probability is near one and grows without bound as it approaches zero, punishing confident wrong predictions hardest.

cross-validation.alt: Diagram of k-fold cross-validation: all data is shown as a row of points, and across iterations 1 to k a different contiguous block is boxed off as the test data while the remaining blocks serve as training data.
cross-validation.caption: K-fold cross-validation rotates the test set instead of relying on one lucky split. The data is cut into k blocks, and in each iteration a different block is held out for testing (boxed) while the rest is used for training. Every example is tested exactly once, and averaging the scores gives a stable estimate of how the model generalizes.

cuda.alt: Three rows mapping a CUDA software abstraction to hardware. A single Thread is executed by a Core; a Thread Block of many threads is executed by a Streaming Multiprocessor; a Kernel Grid of many blocks is executed by the complete GPU.
cuda.caption: CUDA's programming model is a hierarchy that maps directly onto the hardware: a single thread runs on one core, a block of threads runs on one streaming multiprocessor, and the whole grid of blocks runs across the entire GPU. Writing a CUDA kernel means describing the work of one thread, which the hardware then replicates across thousands of threads in parallel.

curvature.alt: A globe with a triangle whose three angles are 90, 90, and 50 degrees, alongside an inset of a small flat triangle whose angles are 90, 50, and 40 degrees.
curvature.caption: Curvature can be detected from inside a space by the angles of a triangle. On the curved surface of the Earth a triangle's angles sum to more than 180 degrees (here 90 plus 90 plus 50), while on a flat patch they sum to exactly 180, so the excess is a direct measure of how the surface bends.

cybernetics.alt: A closed-loop control diagram: a reference input r enters a summing junction, the error e feeds a controller C, whose output u drives a system P to produce output y, which is fed back and subtracted from r.
cybernetics.caption: The feedback loop is the central object of cybernetics. The system compares its output y against a target r, computes the error e, and the controller acts to shrink that error, then senses the result and corrects again. This same loop of acting, sensing, and correcting underlies thermostats, reinforcement learning, and gradient descent.

data-augmentation.alt: One photograph of a rock surface turned into several training variants: a set of random crops in the top row and a horizontally flipped copy in the bottom row, each still showing the same rock.
data-augmentation.caption: Data augmentation manufactures new training examples by transforming existing ones in ways that change appearance but not the label. A single rock photo yields many random crops and a horizontal flip; the network sees far more data than was collected and is pushed to rely on features that survive these transformations rather than memorizing the original.

data-parallelism.alt: A diagram of data parallelism on two GPUs. A full copy of the model goes to GPU 0 and GPU 1, the stochastic mini-batch is split into two subsets (one per GPU), each GPU computes a local gradient from its subset, and the two local gradients are summed into the mini-batch stochastic gradient.
data-parallelism.caption: In data parallelism every GPU holds a complete copy of the model, and the mini-batch is split so each device processes a different subset. Each GPU computes a local gradient on its own data, and the local gradients are summed (an all-reduce) into one mini-batch gradient that updates every replica identically. The model is replicated, not divided, so this scales throughput but not model size.
data-parallelism.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

decision-tree.alt: A decision tree predicting Titanic survival: the root asks if the passenger is male, then branches to questions about age and number of siblings or spouses aboard, ending in leaves labeled died or survived with a probability and the percentage of passengers reaching that leaf.
decision-tree.caption: A decision tree predicts by asking a sequence of simple yes-or-no questions about the features. Following the path from the root (is the passenger male, then how old, then how many siblings aboard) leads to a leaf holding the prediction, here survival or death, with the probability and the share of passengers that reach it.

decoder-only-model.alt: Left: a single Transformer decoder block reads the shifted input <bos> I like this book and outputs I like this book <eos>, one token per position. Right: the attention pattern as a lower-triangular grid, where each output row attends only to the input positions at or before it.
decoder-only-model.caption: A decoder-only model (the GPT design) uses just the decoder stack with causal attention. Each position predicts the next token, so the target is the input shifted by one (left), and the triangular attention pattern (right) lets every position attend only to itself and earlier tokens, never the future. The prompt and the generated text are processed as one continuous sequence.
decoder-only-model.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

deep-learning.alt: A stack of layers reading bottom to top: input photos of animals, then rows of increasingly complex sketched features from short edges, to curves, to body parts, to a whole elephant outline, and finally output labels Penguin, Elephant, Kangaroo.
deep-learning.caption: Deep learning stacks many layers that learn a hierarchy of features directly from raw pixels. Early layers near the bottom capture simple edges, later layers compose them into curves, then parts, then whole objects, until the top layer reads off a label. This learned hierarchy of representations is what makes the network deep.

denoising-diffusion.alt: Six images of a rhinoceros generated with an increasing number of sampling steps, labeled Steps 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, and 150: the leftmost is noisy and malformed and each one to the right is sharper and more coherent.
denoising-diffusion.caption: Denoising diffusion generates by starting from pure noise and walking backward through the noise schedule, using the network's noise prediction to recover a slightly cleaner image at each step. Running more reverse steps (left to right, from 5 up to 150) lets the sampler follow that trajectory more finely, so the rhinoceros resolves from a noisy blur into a coherent picture.

dense-retrieval.alt: A scatter of concept words (car, fuel efficiency, cargo capacity, towing, truck, convertible, Chevrolet, Ford, off-road) placed as points. Two orange vectors are drawn from a labeled Origin out toward two of the points, with an 80 degree angle marked between them.
dense-retrieval.caption: Dense retrieval places queries and documents as points in an embedding space and ranks by closeness, typically the cosine of the angle between their vectors. A smaller angle means more similar meaning, so the score depends on direction in the space rather than on shared words. This is what lets a dense retriever connect related ideas even when the exact vocabulary differs.

dependency-parsing.alt: Two parse diagrams of the sentence 'The girl is happy', side by side. The left is labeled Dependency: words link directly to other words, with 'is' as the top word and 'girl' and 'happy' hanging from it and 'The' under 'girl'. The right is labeled Constituency (BPS): the words are grouped into nested phrase brackets.
dependency-parsing.caption: Dependency parsing (left) connects each word straight to the word it depends on, building a tree of head-and-dependent links with a single root, here the verb 'is'. This is the contrast with constituency parsing (right), which instead groups words into nested phrases. The dependency view exposes who relates to whom, which is closer to meaning.

derivative.alt: Animation of a curve with a straight line touching it at a single moving point; as the point of contact slides along the curve, the line keeps matching the curve's slope there.
derivative.caption: The derivative at a point is the slope of the tangent line that just grazes the curve there. As the point of contact slides along the curve, the tangent's slope changes, and that slope read off at every position is itself the derivative function.

determinant.alt: A parallelogram in the plane with one corner at the origin, spanned by the vectors (a, b) and (c, d). Its fourth corner is at (a plus c, b plus d), and its interior is labeled with the area ad minus bc.
determinant.caption: The determinant of a two-by-two matrix is the signed area of the parallelogram its columns span, here ad minus bc. So it measures how much the transformation scales area (volume in higher dimensions): a determinant of zero means the parallelogram collapses to a line, which is exactly when the matrix is singular and has no inverse.

differential-geometry.alt: Three surfaces colored by curvature: a hyperboloid with a pinched waist (negative curvature), a straight cylinder (zero curvature), and a sphere (positive curvature).
differential-geometry.caption: Curvature is the local quantity differential geometry uses to say how far a space departs from flat. A saddle-like surface that curves in opposite directions has negative curvature (left), a cylinder that bends only one way is intrinsically flat with zero curvature (middle), and a sphere that curves the same way in every direction has positive curvature (right). Measuring length, angle, and curvature on such smooth curved spaces is the heart of the subject.

differential-privacy.alt: Two databases, D1 and D2, that differ only in whether one person's record (Joe's data) is included, each fed through the same analysis M, producing answers A and B that are shown as approximately equal.
differential-privacy.caption: Differential privacy is a property of the analysis, not a hope about the attacker. If two datasets differ in just one individual's record, the analysis must produce nearly indistinguishable outputs, so no observer can tell from the result whether any single person's data was included.
differential-privacy.credit: Joseph Near, David Darais and Kaitlin Boeckl

diffusion-model.alt: A photograph of a rhinoceros shown at successive noise levels from left to right, labeled Z at T equals 0 through Z at T equals n: the clean image at the left is progressively buried under Gaussian noise until the final frame is pure noise.
diffusion-model.caption: A diffusion model is built on a fixed forward process that adds a little Gaussian noise to a real image step by step, from the clean sample at the left (Z at T equals 0) until nothing remains but pure noise (Z at T equals n). The model itself learns only to run this process in reverse, starting from noise and removing a little at a time until a coherent sample reappears.

dimensionality-reduction.alt: Two panels of the same color-graded data: on the left a tangled 2D projection where colors overlap, on the right an unrolled rectangle where the color gradient runs smoothly across the plane.
dimensionality-reduction.caption: Dimensionality reduction seeks honest low-dimensional coordinates for data that lies on a curved manifold. A naive flat projection (left) tangles points that are far apart along the surface, while unrolling the manifold (right) lays it out so the smooth color gradient is preserved, recovering the data's true two-dimensional structure.

directional-derivative.alt: A green surface over a base plane; a direction vector h sits in the plane at a point A, a vertical plane cuts the surface along that direction, and the blue tangent line to the resulting cross-section at the point above A makes an angle alpha with the horizontal.
directional-derivative.caption: The directional derivative is the rate a function changes as you move from a point along a chosen direction. Cutting the surface with a vertical plane through the direction vector h gives a cross-section curve, and the slope of its tangent line at A (the angle alpha) is the directional derivative, equal to the gradient dotted with the unit direction.

dot-product.alt: Two vectors a and b drawn from a common origin with the angle phi between them. A dashed line drops b perpendicularly onto the line of a, marking the scalar projection of b onto a.
dot-product.caption: The dot product equals the lengths of the two vectors times the cosine of the angle phi between them, which is also the length of one vector times the projection of the other onto it. So it is large when the vectors point the same way, zero when they are perpendicular, and negative when they oppose, the measure of alignment behind cosine similarity and attention.

double-descent.alt: A schematic plot of error against increasing model capacity. A dashed train-error curve falls steadily toward zero. A solid test-error curve falls, then rises to a sharp peak at a dashed vertical line marking the interpolation threshold, then falls a second time. The region left of the line is labeled the classical underparametrized regime and the region right of it the modern interpolating overparametrized regime.
double-descent.caption: Double descent breaks the classical U-shaped picture of overfitting. Test error first falls then rises to a peak at the interpolation threshold, where the model has just enough capacity to fit the training data exactly, and then falls a second time as capacity grows further into the overparametrized regime. The lowest test error can sit far to the right, past the point where training error reaches zero.

dpo.alt: Two side-by-side pipelines starting from the same preference data. On the left, labeled RLHF, preference data trains a reward model by maximum likelihood, then sampled completions are labeled with rewards to optimize an LM policy by reinforcement learning. On the right, labeled DPO, preference data optimizes the final language model directly by maximum likelihood with no reward model and no reinforcement learning.
dpo.caption: Direct preference optimization reaches the same goal as RLHF by a simpler route. RLHF (left) first trains a separate reward model, then runs reinforcement learning against it. DPO (right) skips both: a single classification-style loss raises the model's likelihood of the preferred response over the rejected one, learning directly from the same comparison data in one supervised stage.
dpo.credit: Rafael Rafailov, Archit Sharma, Eric Mitchell, Stefano Ermon, Christopher D. Manning, Chelsea Finn

dropout.alt: The same multilayer perceptron shown before and after dropout: on the left all five hidden units and their connections are present, and on the right two hidden units have been removed along with every connection to and from them.
dropout.caption: Dropout randomly zeroes a fraction of a layer's units on each training step, here removing two of the five hidden units. Every step therefore trains a different thinned subnetwork, which stops units from co-adapting and acts like averaging over many networks, reducing overfitting.
dropout.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

early-stopping.alt: Plot of error versus number of training iterations: the training-set error keeps falling while the validation-set error falls, reaches a minimum, then rises, with the minimum marked as the early-stopping point.
early-stopping.caption: As training proceeds the training error keeps falling, but the held-out validation error falls only until the model starts to overfit, then turns and rises. Early stopping ends training at the bottom of the validation curve, keeping the checkpoint with the best generalization.

eigenvalue.alt: The Mona Lisa on the left and a horizontally sheared copy on the right. A red arrow lies along the horizontal direction in both images and keeps its direction, while a blue arrow that was at an angle is tilted by the shear.
eigenvalue.caption: When this shear transformation acts on the painting, the red vector along the horizontal axis keeps pointing the same way and is only rescaled, while the blue vector is rotated. The red direction is an eigenvector, and the factor by which its length changes is the eigenvalue: the pure stretching the matrix applies along that special unchanging direction.

eigenvector.alt: A grid of points in the plane with small arrows attached. As a shear transformation is applied, the red and blue arrows stay along their original lines while the other arrows rotate away from their starting directions.
eigenvector.caption: Under a linear transformation most vectors get both turned and rescaled, but an eigenvector comes out pointing along the very same line it started on. The red and blue arrows here mark those special directions that the shear only stretches and never rotates, the natural axes along which the matrix acts most simply.

embedding-model.alt: A two-dimensional plot with four labeled points: Man and King in blue on the left and upper area, Woman and Queen in red. A dashed arrow runs from Man to Woman and a parallel dashed arrow runs from King to Queen, the two arrows pointing in nearly the same direction.
embedding-model.caption: An embedding model turns meaning into geometry: each word becomes a point, and related words land near each other. The relationship itself becomes a direction, so the offset from Man to Woman is nearly the same vector as the offset from King to Queen. Comparing meaning becomes comparing coordinates, which is the foundation under semantic search and retrieval.

embodied-cognition.alt: A block diagram of a subsumption architecture: a Sensor Information box on the left feeds three parallel behavior layers, Explore World, Wander Around, and Avoid Objects, whose outputs combine into an Actuators box on the right, with a feedback arrow running from the actuators back to the sensors.
embodied-cognition.caption: Rodney Brooks's subsumption architecture builds intelligent behavior from layered perception-to-action loops rather than a central world model. Sensor information drives several simple behaviors in parallel (avoid objects, wander, explore), and their outputs are combined to drive the actuators, whose effects feed back through the body and environment to the sensors. This is the embodied alternative to sense, build a model, then reason, plan, and act.

emergence.alt: Thousands of starlings in flight against an orange dusk sky, their individual bodies forming one large, flowing, coordinated shape above a row of dark trees.
emergence.caption: A starling murmuration is a textbook case of emergence: no bird leads and each follows only simple local rules about its nearest neighbors, yet the flock as a whole forms a single coherent, shifting shape that no individual bird possesses or controls. The pattern is a property of the organized interaction of the parts, not of any part alone.

emergent-ability.alt: A scatter plot of MMLU benchmark performance in percent against scaled training compute on a logarithmic axis, with individual models such as GPT-3, PaLM, PaLM-2 and GPT-4 labeled and a fitted S-shaped curve rising from near 25 percent at low compute to near 90 percent at high compute.
emergent-ability.caption: An emergent ability looks like a capability that switches on with scale: performance sits near the chance floor of about 25 percent for smaller models, then rises steeply once compute crosses a threshold, an S-shaped curve that makes the skill hard to forecast from small models alone.

empirical-risk-minimization.alt: Four small plots of fits to the same points, each annotated with two numbers: the empirical loss on the sample and the true loss. The top row shows a smooth curve with near-zero true loss. The bottom-left plot shows a wild curve with empirical loss zero but true loss about twenty-five, and the bottom-right shows a similar wild curve with both losses high.
empirical-risk-minimization.caption: Empirical risk minimization picks the model with the smallest loss on the training sample, used as a stand-in for the true loss over the whole distribution. The danger is the bottom-left case: a flexible model drives the empirical loss to zero by threading every point, yet its true loss stays high. Low training error guarantees low true error only when the model class is restricted enough.

encoder-decoder.alt: The transformer architecture as two stacks. The encoder on the left takes source embeddings plus positional encoding through repeated blocks of multi-head attention and a positionwise feed-forward network, each with add-and-norm. The decoder on the right takes target embeddings through masked multi-head attention, then multi-head attention that reads the encoder output, then a feed-forward network, ending in a fully connected output layer.
encoder-decoder.caption: The original transformer is an encoder-decoder. The encoder reads the whole input into a representation through stacked self-attention and feed-forward blocks, and the decoder generates the output while attending back to that representation through the middle cross-attention block. This two-stack design with an information bridge between them suits sequence-to-sequence tasks such as translation.
encoder-decoder.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

energy-based-model.alt: A one-dimensional energy landscape with energy on the vertical axis and states on the horizontal axis: a curve with two valleys and a hill between them, a marker descending from a high-energy point via an update step into the nearer valley labeled attractor, with the deeper valley marked as the minimum energy.
energy-based-model.caption: An energy-based model learns a scalar energy over configurations, shaping the landscape so that plausible data sits in the low-energy valleys and everything else sits on the hills. Answering a query then becomes a search for the lowest-energy state: starting from some configuration, the model takes gradient update steps that descend the energy until it settles into a minimum.

entropy.alt: Plot of the entropy of a single binary variable against its probability of being one, forming a curve that peaks at one bit when the probability is one half and falls to zero at both ends.
entropy.caption: Entropy measures average uncertainty. For one coin it is largest, one full bit, when the outcome is a fair fifty-fifty and hardest to predict, and it falls to zero at the certain ends where the result carries no information.

equivariance.alt: A commutative square: the top and bottom edges apply a group action g to sets X and Y, the left and right edges apply a function f from X to Y, and both paths around the square agree.
equivariance.caption: Equivariance is this square commuting: transforming the input by a symmetry g and then applying the function f gives the same result as applying f first and then transforming the output. The structure of the symmetry passes cleanly through the computation instead of being scrambled.

evolutionary-strategy.alt: Six panels labeled Generation 1 to 6, each a contour map of an objective landscape. In each panel a cloud of black sample points is drawn from a distribution shown as an orange ellipse. Across the generations the points concentrate toward the optimum and the orange ellipse shrinks and reorients to align with the landscape.
evolutionary-strategy.caption: An evolution strategy keeps a distribution over candidate solutions, shown here as the orange ellipse, rather than a single point. Each generation it samples a population (black points), keeps the best, and updates the distribution toward them. The covariance matrix adaptation method (CMA-ES) reshapes that ellipse to stretch along productive directions and shrink across unproductive ones, learning the local geometry as the search converges.

expectation.alt: Two right-skewed distributions with vertical lines marking the mode, median, and mean, where the mean sits farthest to the right toward the long tail.
expectation.caption: The expectation, or mean, is the probability-weighted average of every outcome, so on a skewed distribution it is pulled toward the long tail and sits to the right of the mode (the peak) and the median (the middle value).

expectation-maximization.alt: Four scatter plots of the iris dataset (only the first two features) labeled spherical, diag, tied, and full. Points from three classes are colored navy, turquoise, and orange, with training points as dots and test points as crosses. Each plot overlays three shaded Gaussian ellipses, one per component, whose shapes differ across the four covariance settings, and each plot lists a train and test accuracy.
expectation-maximization.caption: Expectation maximization fits a Gaussian mixture by recovering both the shape of each component and a soft assignment of points to components. Here the same data is fitted under four covariance assumptions, from a single shared variance (spherical) to unrestricted ellipses (full); the fitted component ellipses are what EM estimates, and the simpler settings often generalize better to the held-out test crosses.

f1-score.alt: A precision-recall curve, colored by decision threshold, with a red dot marking the point of maximum F-score on the curve.
f1-score.caption: As the decision threshold sweeps, a classifier traces a precision-recall curve; the F1 score is the harmonic mean of the two, and the red dot marks the threshold where it is maximized. Because the harmonic mean is pulled toward the smaller value, that best point sits where precision and recall are high together, not where one is traded away for the other.

feature-pyramid.alt: A pyramid of an image at successive scales, from Level 0 (original resolution) at the base up to Level 4 (one sixteenth resolution) at the apex, each higher level produced by blurring and subsampling the one below.
feature-pyramid.caption: Scale variation is solved by representing an image at several resolutions at once. Each level is the one below blurred and subsampled, so fine, high-resolution maps near the base catch small objects and coarse maps near the apex catch large ones. A feature pyramid reads predictions from multiple levels for this reason.

feature-visualization.alt: On the left, a synthetic image produced by optimizing the input to maximally activate a neuron, showing an abstract dog-like motif; on the right, a grid of real photographs of dogs that activate the same neuron most strongly.
feature-visualization.caption: Feature visualization reveals what a unit detects in two complementary ways. On the left, the input is optimized from noise until it maximally activates the neuron, synthesizing the abstract pattern it most wants to see; on the right, the real images that excite it most are collected from a dataset. Both point to the same learned concept, here a dog, making an opaque neuron legible.
feature-visualization.credit: Olah, Cammarata, Schubert, Goh, Petrov, Carter

federated-learning.alt: A central server at the top labeled as coordinating the training of a global AI model, with double-headed arrows down to four phones at the bottom, each holding a local AI model.
federated-learning.caption: In federated learning the data never leaves the device. A central server sends the shared model out to many participants, each trains it on its own local data, and only the resulting updates flow back up to be averaged into an improved global model. The two-way arrows are model parameters moving, not raw data.

feed-forward-network.alt: The transformer feed-forward module. An input vector of size d-emb passes through a linear layer that expands it to a wider hidden size d-ffn, then an activation function, then a second linear layer that projects it back down to d-emb. A compact symbol on the right abbreviates the whole block as FFN.
feed-forward-network.caption: The feed-forward network in a transformer block is a two-layer network applied to each position on its own. The first linear layer expands the token's representation from the model width d-emb to a wider hidden size d-ffn, an activation adds nonlinearity, and the second linear layer projects it back to d-emb. The same weights are reused at every position, so it mixes features within a token rather than across tokens.

few-shot-learning.alt: Three prompting setups feeding a Transformer decoder marked no parameter update. The lower few-shot panel places several worked translation examples (go maps to va, i lost maps to j'ai perdu, he's calm maps to elle court) between the task description and the real prompt, contrasted with the zero-shot and one-shot panels above.
few-shot-learning.caption: Few-shot learning puts a handful of worked examples in the prompt (the few-shot panel) so the model infers the pattern and applies it to the new input. No weights change (the box is marked no parameter update); the examples alone supply the task, which often matches fine-tuning without any training. Zero-shot and one-shot are the same idea with no example or just one.
few-shot-learning.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

fine-tuning.alt: Two stacks of layers side by side. The source model, trained on a source dataset, has layer 1 up to an output layer. Arrows labeled copy carry the lower layers across to a target model trained on a target dataset, whose output layer is instead randomly initialized and trained from scratch, with the copied layers marked as fine-tuned.
fine-tuning.caption: Fine-tuning reuses a model already pretrained on a large source dataset. Its learned feature layers are copied into a target model and adapted on the smaller target data, while only a fresh output layer is trained from scratch. The pretrained weights give a strong starting point, so the target task succeeds with far less data than learning from random initialization would need.
fine-tuning.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

fisher-information.alt: Two nested ellipses around a central true parameter point: a large outer ellipse labeled an arbitrary unbiased estimator and a small inner ellipse labeled the estimator at the Cramer-Rao lower bound.
fisher-information.caption: The Fisher information sets the best possible precision of estimation. The Cramer-Rao bound says no unbiased estimator can have variance smaller than the inverse Fisher information, so the tight inner ellipse is the smallest spread any estimator of the true parameter can achieve, while a worse estimator scatters across the wide outer ellipse.

flash-attention.alt: Three panels. Left: a memory pyramid with fast small SRAM (19 TB/s, 20 MB) on top, larger slower HBM (1.5 TB/s, 40 GB) below, and large slow CPU DRAM at the base. Middle: blocks of the query, key, and value matrices copied into SRAM where the attention block is computed, with the output written back to HBM, never storing the full score matrix. Right: a bar chart showing the fused FlashAttention kernel is much faster than standard PyTorch attention.
flash-attention.caption: FlashAttention exploits the GPU memory hierarchy: SRAM is tiny but fast, HBM is large but slow (left). Standard attention writes the full pairwise score matrix to slow HBM and reads it back; FlashAttention instead loads blocks of Q, K, and V into fast SRAM and computes each tile there, never materializing that matrix (middle). The result is exactly the same output, produced far faster as a fused kernel (right).
flash-attention.credit: Tri Dao, Daniel Y. Fu, Stefano Ermon, Atri Rudra, Christopher Re

flat-minima.alt: A three-dimensional rendering of a neural network loss surface that is smooth and gently curved, descending into a single wide bowl-shaped basin.
flat-minima.caption: A flat minimum sits in a wide, low-curvature basin like this one: the weights can be perturbed a long way before the loss climbs, which makes the solution robust to the small shift from training data to test data and is empirically linked to better generalization.
flat-minima.credit: Hao Li, Zheng Xu, Gavin Taylor, Christoph Studer, Tom Goldstein

fourier-transform.alt: Animation building a red square wave in the time domain by adding sinusoids one at a time, with a second axis showing a frequency-domain spectrum that grows a sharp spike at each frequency as its sinusoid is added.
fourier-transform.caption: The Fourier transform re-expresses a signal as a sum of pure oscillations. As sinusoids of higher frequency are added, they build up the square wave in the time domain (red), and each one appears as a sharp spike in the frequency-domain spectrum. The transform moves between these two views, from how a signal evolves in time to what frequencies it is made of.

frank-wolfe.alt: A convex objective f drawn as a blue bowl sitting above a hexagonal feasible region D. A tilted plane touches the bowl at the current point f(x), representing the linear approximation of the objective, and a green arrow labeled x points across the feasible region toward an extreme point s of the hexagon.
frank-wolfe.caption: The Frank-Wolfe algorithm avoids projection by linearizing. At the current point it replaces the convex objective f with its tangent linear approximation (the tilted plane), minimizes that linear function over the feasible region D, which always lands on a vertex s of the set, and then steps from the current point toward that vertex. Every iterate stays feasible automatically, with no projection needed.

free-energy-principle.alt: A diagram applying the same scheme to a cell and a brain. States are split into hidden (external) states, sensory states, active states, and internal states. For the brain, internal states change to minimize free energy F with respect to sensations (perception), and active states change F with respect to the world (action).
free-energy-principle.caption: The free energy principle says a system can lower the surprise of its sensations in two ways, shown by the two equations: change internal states to better explain the input (perception and learning), or change active states to make the world match the prediction (action). This single objective, minimizing variational free energy F, folds perception and action together into what is called active inference.

free-will.alt: A decision tree of positions on free will. The first question, is determinism true, branches to a second question, is there free will. The answers lead to compatibilism, or into a shaded region labeled incompatibilism that contains hard determinism and libertarianism.
free-will.caption: The free-will debate is organized by two questions. Compatibilists say free will and determinism can both hold. Incompatibilists deny this, splitting into hard determinists, who accept determinism and conclude free will is an illusion, and libertarians, who keep free will and reject determinism. The tree shows that one's stance is fixed by whether determinism is true and whether that rules out genuine freedom.

fully-sharded-data-parallel.alt: A diagram decomposing an all-reduce into a reduce-scatter followed by an all-gather across four GPUs. On the left, all-reduce leaves every GPU with the full summed result. In the middle, reduce-scatter leaves each GPU with only its own reduced slice. On the right, all-gather rebuilds the full set of slices on every GPU.
fully-sharded-data-parallel.caption: Fully sharded data parallel shards the parameters, gradients, and optimizer state so each GPU stores only a slice. As shown, the all-reduce of standard data parallelism is split into a reduce-scatter (each device keeps just its averaged slice) and an all-gather (the full weights are reassembled only when a layer is about to run, then discarded). Peak memory is set by the largest single layer, not the whole model.

gated-recurrent-unit.alt: Diagram of a gated recurrent unit: the previous hidden state and current input feed a reset gate and an update gate (both sigmoid layers) and a candidate state (a tanh layer), and elementwise operators combine them so the update gate interpolates between the old hidden state and the candidate to produce the new hidden state.
gated-recurrent-unit.caption: A gated recurrent unit controls its memory with two learned gates. The reset gate decides how much past state shapes a tanh candidate state, and the update gate interpolates between the old hidden state and that candidate to form the new one. When the update gate preserves the state, the path across time is near identity, so gradients survive over long sequences.
gated-recurrent-unit.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

gaussian-curvature.alt: Three shaded surfaces side by side: a saddle-shaped surface of negative curvature, a cylinder of zero curvature, and a sphere of positive curvature.
gaussian-curvature.caption: Gaussian curvature is the product of the two principal curvatures, and its sign classifies a surface point. A saddle bends in opposite directions and has negative curvature (left), a cylinder is flat in one direction and has zero curvature (center), and a sphere bows the same way in every direction and has positive curvature (right).

gaussian-distribution.alt: Plot of normal distribution probability density curves for several values of mean and variance
gaussian-distribution.caption: The bell curve is fixed by its mean (where it centers) and its variance (how wide it spreads); nearly all mass falls within a few standard deviations.

gaussian-process.alt: Three panels labeled Prior, Posterior, and Prediction with Uncertainty. The Prior shows several wiggly random functions filling the plot. The Posterior shows similar functions but all passing through a set of fixed data points. The Prediction panel shows a single mean curve with a grey shaded band around it that is narrow at the data points and wide in the gaps between them.
gaussian-process.caption: A Gaussian process is a distribution over functions. The prior is a spread of candidate functions (left); conditioning on observed points keeps only functions that pass through the data (center); and the prediction reports a mean function with an uncertainty band that is narrow near the data and widens far from it (right). The widening band is the model reporting what it does not know.

gaussian-splatting.alt: The 3D Gaussian splatting pipeline: structure-from-motion points initialize a set of 3D Gaussians, which are projected with a camera pose and passed through a differentiable tile rasterizer to render an image, with adaptive density control and gradient flow refining the Gaussians.
gaussian-splatting.caption: Gaussian splatting stores a scene explicitly as many 3D Gaussian blobs rather than in a network's weights. Starting from sparse structure-from-motion points, the Gaussians are projected for a given camera and rasterized into an image; gradients from the rendered image then refine each Gaussian's position, shape, color, and opacity, and adaptive density control adds or removes blobs.
gaussian-splatting.credit: Bernhard Kerbl, Georgios Kopanas, Thomas Leimkuhler, George Drettakis

generalization.alt: Three scatter plots of the same points fitted by three curves. The left panel, labeled underfitting, fits a straight line that misses the curvature of the data. The middle panel fits a smooth curve that follows the overall trend. The right panel, labeled overfitting, fits a wiggly curve that passes through nearly every point.
generalization.caption: Generalization is about fitting the signal without chasing the noise. A model that is too simple underfits and misses the real pattern (left); a model that is too flexible overfits, bending to hit every training point and swinging wildly between them (right). The middle fit captures the underlying trend and is the one most likely to do well on new data.

generative-adversarial-network.alt: Schematic of a generative adversarial network showing the generator and discriminator networks competing
generative-adversarial-network.caption: A GAN pits a generator that fabricates samples against a discriminator that judges real from fake; their competition drives both to improve.
generative-adversarial-network.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

geodesic.alt: A sphere with a great circle drawn in red and a smaller parallel circle in blue, showing that the geodesic on a sphere is the great circle.
geodesic.caption: On a flat plane the shortest path between two points is a straight line; on a curved surface it is a geodesic. On a sphere every geodesic is a great circle (red), the largest circle through two points, so a small circle (blue) is always the longer detour.

gibbs-sampling.alt: Three density curves labeled Step 1, Step 2, and Step K, with curved arrows leading from one to the next and wrapping back around. Under each step is the full conditional it samples from, written as theta-k drawn from pi of theta-k given the other thetas and the data.
gibbs-sampling.caption: Gibbs sampling draws from a hard joint distribution by updating one variable at a time. In each step it samples a single variable from its full conditional given the current values of all the others, then sweeps to the next variable and repeats. The cycle of conditional draws (Step 1, Step 2, on to Step K and back) produces a chain whose stationary distribution is the target joint.

goal-misgeneralization.alt: Two side-by-side frames of the CoinRun platformer game: (a) goal position fixed, with the coin at the end of the level, and (b) goal position randomized, with the coin moved elsewhere while the agent still heads to the end of the level.
goal-misgeneralization.caption: An agent trained where the coin always sits at the end of the level (a) learns to run to the end, a goal indistinguishable from collecting the coin on the training data. When the coin is moved at test time (b) the agent keeps running to the end and skips the coin: its navigation skill transfers but the wrong goal does too. The capability generalizes while the objective does not.
goal-misgeneralization.credit: Lauro Langosco, Jack Koch, Lee Sharkey, Jacob Pfau, Laurent Orseau, David Krueger

gpu.alt: Two side-by-side silicon floorplans. The CPU devotes most of its area to a large Control unit, a big Cache, and only four ALUs. The GPU spends almost all its area on a dense grid of many small ALUs, with little control logic or cache.
gpu.caption: A CPU spends most of its transistors on control logic and cache to run a few threads fast, while a GPU spends almost all of its area on a large grid of arithmetic units (ALUs). This is why a GPU is so well matched to deep learning: the dense matrix math of a neural network is thousands of identical multiply-add operations that can run on those many cores at once.

gradient.alt: A color heat map of a scalar function with a yellow high region and a purple low region, overlaid with black arrows; the arrows point from low toward high and are longest where the color changes fastest.
gradient.caption: The gradient collects a function's partial derivatives into a vector that points in the direction of steepest increase. Overlaid on a heat map of the function, each arrow points from the low (purple) toward the high (yellow) values, and its length is largest where the function changes most steeply.

gradient-boosting.alt: Line plot of mean squared error against the number of boosting iterations, dropping steeply over the first hundred iterations from about thirteen thousand toward two thousand, then flattening for the remaining iterations.
gradient-boosting.caption: Gradient boosting adds simple trees one at a time, each fit to the errors the ensemble has not yet corrected. The training error falls sharply at first as each new tree fixes what the others missed, then flattens as little error remains. Adding trees indefinitely risks overfitting, which the learning rate and early stopping hold in check.

gradient-checkpointing.alt: A feed-forward network drawn as a computation graph: a top row of forward-pass nodes feeds a bottom row of backward-pass gradient nodes. One node in the forward row is drawn with a double circle and labeled checkpoint.
gradient-checkpointing.caption: Gradient checkpointing saves only a few activations, the checkpoint nodes (double-circled here), during the forward pass and discards the rest. When the backward pass needs a discarded activation, it recomputes it from the nearest checkpoint rather than having stored it. This trades extra computation for much lower memory, turning activation memory that grew with depth into memory that grows with roughly its square root.

gradient-descent.alt: Animation frame showing gradient descent stepping downhill toward the minimum of a 2D loss surface
gradient-descent.caption: Gradient descent steps downhill on the loss surface, each move pointing along the steepest local descent, until it settles into a minimum.

graph-attention-network.alt: A central node h1 surrounded by neighbors h2 through h6, with a learned attention coefficient alpha on each incoming edge; multiple colored arrows per edge denote attention heads, combined by concatenation or averaging into the updated node h1 prime.
graph-attention-network.caption: A graph attention network learns a weight for each edge instead of fixing it by graph structure. Node h1 scores each neighbor with an attention coefficient alpha, then takes the attention-weighted sum of their features; the several colored arrows per edge are independent attention heads, concatenated or averaged to form the updated representation h1 prime.

graph-convolution.alt: A graph convolutional layer: a graph at Layer N with node, edge, and global attributes U, V, E enters; a pooling function rho gathers each node's one-hop neighborhood and an update function f is applied; the same graph with new attributes exits as Layer N+1.
graph-convolution.caption: A graph convolutional layer updates each node by pooling the features of its one-hop neighbors (the dotted neighborhood) and passing the result through a small learned update function f. Stacking such layers spreads information one hop further each time, the graph counterpart of sliding a convolution filter over a pixel grid.
