graph-neural-network.alt: Three graph neural network building blocks left to right: a permutation-equivariant layer that updates each node from its neighbors and colors the nodes, a local pooling layer that coarsens the graph to fewer nodes, and a global readout that collapses the whole graph to a single node.
graph-neural-network.caption: A graph neural network is built from three kinds of layer. A permutation-equivariant layer updates every node by aggregating its neighbors (the coloring), optional local pooling coarsens the graph, and a global readout pools all nodes into one fixed-size vector for graph-level prediction. Node, edge, and whole-graph tasks all read off these same learned node representations.

graphical-model.alt: A directed graph of three nodes. RAIN points to both SPRINKLER and GRASS WET, and SPRINKLER also points to GRASS WET. Beside each node is a conditional probability table: RAIN's table is unconditional, SPRINKLER's is conditioned on RAIN, and GRASS WET's is conditioned on both SPRINKLER and RAIN.
graphical-model.caption: A graphical model represents a joint distribution as a graph of variables, with each node carrying only its conditional probability given its parents. Here whether the grass is wet depends on the sprinkler and the rain, and the sprinkler depends on the rain. The missing arrow from rain directly bypassing the sprinkler encodes conditional independence, which lets the full joint be stored as a few small local tables instead of one huge one.

greedy-decoding.alt: A grid with four columns labeled time steps 1 to 4 and rows for tokens A, B, C, and end-of-sequence. Each cell holds that token's probability at that step, and the single highest-probability cell in each column is shaded: 0.5, then 0.4, then 0.4, then 0.6.
greedy-decoding.caption: Greedy decoding takes the single highest-probability token at every step, shown by the shaded cell in each column (A, then B, then C, then end-of-sequence). It never looks ahead or reconsiders, so it is fast and deterministic but can commit to a locally best token that leads to a worse overall sequence.
greedy-decoding.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

grokking.alt: A plot of log loss against training epoch for a network learning modular addition: the training loss (blue) drops to near zero almost immediately, while the test loss (red) stays high for thousands of epochs and then falls sharply to near zero between roughly ten thousand and fifteen thousand epochs.
grokking.caption: Grokking is delayed generalization. Training loss (blue) collapses almost at once as the network memorizes the data, yet test loss (red) stays high for thousands of further epochs and only then plunges to near zero, the moment the network replaces its memorized lookup with a true algorithmic circuit. Generalization arrives long after the training set is already fit.

group-theory.alt: Eight nodes arranged in two nested squares, each node showing the letter F in a different orientation; red arrows connect nodes by a rotation and blue edges connect them by a reflection, labeled with group elements e, a, a-squared, a-cubed, b, and their products.
group-theory.caption: The symmetry group of a shape, drawn as a Cayley graph. Each of the eight nodes is one symmetry of the letter F (the identity e, the three rotations a, a-squared, a-cubed, and four reflections), and following a red arrow applies the rotation generator while a blue edge applies the reflection. The picture shows a group concretely: a closed set of transformations that compose and undo, which is what group theory studies as symmetry.
group-theory.credit: Created by Denisgomes, 2007 Modified by Watchduck, 2011

grouped-query-attention.alt: Three attention layouts compared. Multi-head on the left gives every query column its own key and value. Grouped-query in the middle lets each group of query columns share one key and one value. Multi-query on the right has all query columns share a single key and value.
grouped-query-attention.caption: Grouped-query attention (middle) is the compromise between multi-head attention (left), where every query head has its own key and value, and multi-query attention (right), where all heads share one key and value. By giving each group of query heads a shared key and value, it shrinks the KV cache and memory bandwidth that dominate inference while keeping quality close to full multi-head attention.
grouped-query-attention.credit: Joshua Ainslie et al. (GQA paper, arXiv:2305.13245)

hebbian-learning.alt: A plot of synaptic change against the time between the presynaptic and postsynaptic spike. When the presynaptic spike comes first the synapse strengthens (potentiation, red, left), and when it comes after the postsynaptic spike it weakens (depression, blue, right), within a short time window.
hebbian-learning.caption: Spike-timing-dependent plasticity is the precise modern form of Hebbian learning. The synapse strengthens when the input spike arrives just before the neuron fires (potentiation, left) and weakens when it arrives just after (depression, right), so the rule encodes causal order, not mere coincidence. The two French labels read potentiation and depression.

hegelian-dialectic.alt: A diagram with three boxes: Thesis linked to Antithesis, both feeding a Synthesis box below, with a numbered arrow from the synthesis back up to the thesis position.
hegelian-dialectic.caption: The dialectic moves in stages: a thesis (1) generates its opposing antithesis (2), and the tension between them is resolved in a synthesis (3) that preserves and lifts up both. The arrow back to the top shows the process repeating, the synthesis becoming a new thesis, so development proceeds through the productive resolution of contradiction rather than by simple accumulation.

hessian.alt: A curved three-dimensional surface over the x and y plane bearing several marked critical points, including a local maximum, a local minimum, and a saddle where the surface curves up one way and down the other.
hessian.caption: The Hessian is the matrix of second partial derivatives, and it encodes a function's local curvature. At a point where the gradient is zero, the signs of the Hessian's eigenvalues classify the surface: all positive gives a local minimum, all negative a local maximum, and mixed signs a saddle point.

hidden-markov-model.alt: A diagram unrolled in time. A top row of hidden state nodes x(t-1), x(t), x(t+1) is connected by left-to-right arrows forming a chain, and each hidden state has a downward arrow to its own observation node y(t-1), y(t), y(t+1) in a bottom row.
hidden-markov-model.caption: A hidden Markov model unrolled over time. The hidden state x evolves as a Markov chain, where each state depends only on the previous one (the top arrows), and at every step the current state emits a visible observation y (the downward arrows). You see only the bottom row and must infer the hidden top row that produced it.

hilbert-space.alt: A point x above a plane E, with a vertical segment dropping to its foot P_E(x) on the plane; the segment meets the plane at a marked right angle, and dashed lines show x projected onto two lines within the plane.
hilbert-space.caption: A Hilbert space keeps the geometry of ordinary Euclidean space (length, angle, and projection) even in infinite dimensions. The key property pictured here is orthogonal projection: the projection P_E(x) of a point x onto a subspace E is the unique closest point in E, and the leftover vector from x to P_E(x) meets the subspace at a right angle. This is the same projection that underlies least squares and Fourier expansions.

hnsw.alt: Three panels of the same set of points. Layer 2 (Sparse) holds a few points with long-range links, an entry point, and a path toward the red query vector via its nearest neighbor. Layer 1 (Intermediate) has more points, and Layer 0 (Dense, All Points) holds every point. Arrows labeled descend connect the panels left to right.
hnsw.caption: HNSW stacks the vectors into layers and searches by hopping toward the query. The sparse top layer has a few long-range links that cover huge distances in a few steps, and a search descends through progressively denser layers, refining the result until it lands in the true local neighborhood of the query (red). This layered, greedy navigation is what gives the index its logarithmic-like scaling.

hyena.alt: Diagram of the Hyena operator drawn as a recurrence: an input u is projected, then alternately passed through long-convolution matrices labeled S (shown as banded Toeplitz matrices) and data-controlled diagonal gating matrices labeled D, repeated several times to the output y; on the right, the long filters are generated by a small feed-forward network with a window and positional encoding.
hyena.caption: Hyena replaces self-attention with a recurrence of two cheap primitives applied in alternation: very long convolutions (the Toeplitz matrices S, giving a global receptive field) and element-wise multiplicative gating controlled by the input (the diagonal matrices D). The convolution filters are not stored explicitly but generated by a small feed-forward network from each position (right), which keeps the parameter count independent of sequence length and lets the long convolutions run efficiently with the fast Fourier transform.

hyperbolic-embeddings.alt: A disk filled with a tiling of triangles that appear large at the center and shrink rapidly toward the circular boundary, never reaching it.
hyperbolic-embeddings.caption: Hyperbolic space, drawn here as the Poincare disk, has room that grows exponentially toward its boundary. Every triangle in this tiling is actually the same hyperbolic size; they only look smaller near the rim because distance there is far larger than it appears, and that extra room is what lets exponentially branching trees and hierarchies embed with low distortion.

hyperparameter-optimization.alt: A two-dimensional hyperparameter space with sampled configurations marked as crosses scattered over a contour map of model performance, and rug marks along each axis showing the distinct values tried for that hyperparameter.
hyperparameter-optimization.caption: Random search picks hyperparameter configurations at random across the space rather than on a fixed grid. Because each trial uses a fresh value on every axis (the rug marks along the edges), random search explores many distinct settings of each hyperparameter for the same budget, which finds good configurations faster than grid search when only a few hyperparameters truly matter.

image-classification.alt: Diagram of a convolutional network classifying an image: an input picture passes through convolutional and pooling layers, is flattened, runs through fully connected layers, and outputs class probabilities Rabbit 0.7, Horse 0.3, Pig 0.2.
image-classification.caption: Image classification compresses a whole image down to one labeled decision. Convolutional and pooling layers learn features, the result is flattened and passed through connected layers, and a final layer scores each candidate category, here reading the picture as a rabbit with probability 0.7.

in-context-learning.alt: Three prompting setups, each feeding a Transformer decoder marked no parameter update. Zero-shot gives only a task description and the prompt. One-shot adds a single worked example. Few-shot adds several worked examples. All three produce the same translated output.
in-context-learning.caption: In-context learning is the model learning a task from its prompt at inference time, with no parameter update (note the label on every box). Given a task description and zero, one, or several worked examples before the real prompt, the model infers the pattern and applies it. Adding examples in the context, not retraining, is what steers the output.
in-context-learning.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

inductive-bias.alt: A scatter of noisy data following a wavy periodic pattern, overlaid with a true curve and the fits of three different regression models. The fits agree closely where data is dense but diverge from one another in the region to the right where there are fewer points, each extrapolating in a different way.
inductive-bias.caption: Each model carries an inductive bias, a built-in assumption about what the unseen data looks like. Here several regression methods fit the same noisy points well where data is plentiful but extrapolate differently where it is sparse, because each one's prior (for example a periodic kernel) fills the gaps in its own way. The data alone cannot decide between them; the assumptions do.

information-bottleneck.alt: Two overlapping circles for the entropies H(X) and H(Y); the overlap is labeled the mutual information I(X;Y), the non-overlapping parts are the conditional entropies H(X|Y) and H(Y|X), and the union is the joint entropy H(X,Y).
information-bottleneck.caption: The information bottleneck is stated in the currency this diagram shows: a representation should keep high mutual information with the target (the central overlap I) while squeezing out the input information it does not need, trading compression of the input against prediction of the output.

instance-segmentation.alt: The same photo of balloons shown under four vision tasks: classification (one label), semantic segmentation (all balloons one color), object detection (a labeled box per balloon), and instance segmentation (a distinct colored mask and box for each balloon).
instance-segmentation.caption: The four core vision tasks on one scene. Classification gives a single label; semantic segmentation paints every balloon the same class color; object detection draws a box per object; instance segmentation, bottom right, gives each individual balloon its own pixel-accurate mask, separating two balloons of the same class as distinct instances.

instruction-tuning.alt: A worked instruction-tuning task. A boxed Definition states the task in natural language, a Positive Examples box lists input-output pairs with explanations as demonstrations, and an Evaluation Instances box shows new inputs for which the model produces outputs, illustrating a single task expressed entirely as a written instruction plus examples.
instruction-tuning.caption: Instruction tuning trains a model on many tasks each expressed as a natural-language instruction: a definition plus a few demonstration examples, paired with the desired responses. Trained across a broad mix of such tasks, the model learns to read a prompt as a command to carry out and generalizes to unseen instruction types it was never explicitly shown, the behavior that turns a base model into a usable assistant.
instruction-tuning.credit: Yizhong Wang, Swaroop Mishra, Yeganeh Kordi, et al.

integral.alt: Animation of a curve with the region beneath it filled by a row of rectangles whose tops reach the curve; as the animation proceeds the rectangles grow narrower and their combined area closes in on the true area under the curve.
integral.caption: A definite integral is the area between a curve and the horizontal axis. Approximating that area by a row of rectangles and then letting their width shrink toward zero, as in this animation, makes the staircase of rectangles converge to the exact area, which is the limit that defines the integral.

jacobian.alt: On the left a regular square grid with a red unit square and two basis arrows; a map f sends them to a curved, deformed grid on the right where the red region is stretched and rotated and the two arrows now span a parallelogram.
jacobian.caption: The Jacobian is the matrix of first partial derivatives that best approximates a vector-valued map by a linear one near a point. It sends the square grid on the left to the warped grid on the right, and the factor by which it scales the area of the small red region is its determinant.

jailbreak.alt: Flow diagram: an attacker supplies an adversarial prompt that jailbreaks an LLM, the model returns a response, and that response is meant to match the attacker's desired output, which is the attack goal.
jailbreak.caption: A jailbreak is an adversarial prompt that slips past a model's refusal training to reach the capability underneath. The attacker crafts an input that steers the model's response toward content it was trained to refuse, so the output matches the attacker's goal rather than the safety policy.

jensens-inequality.alt: An upward-curving function with three of its points joined by chords; a dashed vertical segment shows that the averaged point on the chords sits directly above the point on the curve at the same averaged input.
jensens-inequality.caption: Jensen's inequality says that for a convex function the function of an average is at most the average of the function. Because the chords joining points on the curve lie above it, the averaged height on the chords (top of the dashed line) exceeds the curve's height at the averaged input (bottom), which is the gap that drives the evidence lower bound and the non-negativity of KL divergence.

k-means.alt: Animation of k-means clustering: data points colored by their assigned cluster, with two centers that move each iteration as points are reassigned to the nearest center, until the assignments stop changing.
k-means.caption: K-means alternates two steps until it settles. Each point is assigned to the nearest cluster center, then every center is recomputed as the mean of the points now assigned to it. Repeating these steps, shown here over successive iterations, steadily reduces the within-cluster spread and converges to a stable partition.

k-nearest-neighbors.alt: A green query point surrounded by red triangles and blue squares. A solid inner circle encloses two red triangles and one blue square; a dashed outer circle encloses three blue squares and two red triangles.
k-nearest-neighbors.caption: To classify the green point, k-nearest neighbors looks at its closest labeled examples and takes their majority vote. With k equal to 3 (solid circle) the two red triangles win; with k equal to 5 (dashed circle) the three blue squares win. The single choice of k can flip the verdict, which makes it the method's key knob.

kalman-filter.alt: Diagram illustrating the predict and update cycle of the Kalman filter estimating a system state over time
kalman-filter.caption: The Kalman filter alternates predict and update: it projects the state forward, then corrects that estimate with each noisy measurement to track the truth.

kernel-fusion.alt: A transformer layer's operations drawn as a dataflow graph, with dashed boxes grouping consecutive operations into fused regions: a QKV box around the input normalization and the query, key, and value projections; an attention box; an MLP box around the feed-forward layers and activation; and a bias-add box. Each fused region is annotated with a speedup such as 1.5x, 2.9x, 3x.
kernel-fusion.caption: Kernel fusion merges a chain of small operations into one GPU kernel. Here consecutive steps of a transformer layer (the QKV projections, the attention math, the feed-forward block) are each grouped into a single fused kernel instead of many separate ones. Because the intermediate results stay in fast on-chip memory rather than being written to and read back from main memory, each fused region runs several times faster even though the arithmetic is unchanged.

kernel-method.alt: Two panels: on the left, red points clustered inside a ring of purple points in two dimensions, not separable by a straight line; on the right, the same points lifted into three dimensions where the red points sit lower and a flat plane can separate them.
kernel-method.caption: Data that no straight line can separate in its original space (left) often becomes linearly separable after being mapped into a higher dimension (right). The kernel trick computes the needed inner products in that richer space directly from the original points, so a linear method can draw a nonlinear boundary without ever building the high-dimensional coordinates.

kl-divergence.alt: Two bar charts side by side over the outcomes 0, 1, 2: distribution P is a skewed binomial, distribution Q is flat and uniform.
kl-divergence.caption: KL divergence measures how far one distribution is from another. It is the extra cost, in bits, of encoding data drawn from the true distribution P using a code built for the model distribution Q, and it is zero only when the two match.

knowledge-argument.alt: A cartoon of Mary, drawn entirely in grey, stepping out of a grey doorway into a vivid color world of green grass, a tree with a brown trunk, a blue sky, and red and yellow flowers, looking surprised.
knowledge-argument.caption: Mary's Room dramatizes the knowledge argument. The grey scientist knew every physical fact about color while confined to a black-and-white room, yet on stepping out and seeing color for the first time she seems to learn something genuinely new, what red is actually like. If complete physical knowledge still leaves that out, the argument concludes, the felt quality of experience is not captured by the physical facts.

knowledge-distillation.alt: A flow diagram. An input x feeds both a teacher model and a smaller student model. The teacher's logits pass through a softmax with temperature to give soft labels. The student produces soft predictions at the same temperature and a hard prediction at temperature one; a distillation loss compares the student's soft predictions to the teacher's soft labels, and a student loss compares the hard prediction to the ground-truth label.
knowledge-distillation.caption: Knowledge distillation trains a small student model to imitate a large teacher rather than learning only from hard labels. The teacher's full probability distribution, softened with a temperature, becomes a soft target that carries which wrong answers are nearly right. The student minimizes a distillation loss against those soft labels, often blended with the ordinary loss on the true label, inheriting much of the teacher's competence in a smaller network.

kto.alt: A value function plotted with loss on the left and gain on the right of a horizontal axis crossing a marked reference point. The Kahneman-Tversky curve is concave for gains and steeper for losses (loss aversion), with the DPO and PPO-Clip implied value functions drawn for comparison.
kto.caption: KTO borrows the prospect theory value function of Kahneman and Tversky: value is measured relative to a reference point, gains are concave, and losses loom larger than equal gains (loss aversion). KTO scores each response by how far its likelihood has moved from a frozen reference model, then applies this asymmetric value shape, so it can learn from individual responses labeled simply desirable or undesirable rather than from paired comparisons.
kto.credit: Kawin Ethayarajh, Winnie Xu, Niklas Muennighoff, Dan Jurafsky, Douwe Kiela

lagrange-multiplier.alt: Concentric dashed level curves of an objective function f, each with small gradient arrows pointing inward, crossed by a red constraint curve g equals c; the red and dashed curves are tangent where the constrained optimum sits.
lagrange-multiplier.caption: The geometry behind Lagrange multipliers. The dashed curves are level sets of the objective f, and the red curve is the constraint g equals c. At the constrained optimum the constraint curve is tangent to a level curve, so the two gradients line up; the multiplier is the scalar that expresses how much one gradient must be scaled to equal the other. Where the curves merely cross, you can still slide along the constraint to a better value.

late-interaction.alt: A diagram of ColBERT. A Query Encoder produces a row of green per-token vectors and a Document Encoder produces a grid of blue per-token vectors (marked offline indexing). For each query token, a MaxSim operator selects its best-matching document token, and the MaxSim values are summed into a single score.
late-interaction.caption: Late interaction keeps a vector for every token instead of pooling each text to one vector. The document tokens (blue) are encoded and indexed offline; at query time each query token (green) finds its best-matching document token via MaxSim, and those best matches are summed into the relevance score. Doing the interaction late, after encoding, preserves term-level detail while keeping the document side precomputable.

latent-diffusion.alt: Block diagram of the latent diffusion architecture with three regions: a pink Pixel Space where an encoder E maps an image x to a latent z and a decoder D reconstructs it, a green Latent Space where the diffusion and denoising U-Net operate on the latent with cross-attention, and a Conditioning column feeding text, images, and semantic maps in through an encoder.
latent-diffusion.caption: Latent diffusion runs the whole diffusion process in the small latent space of an autoencoder instead of on raw pixels. An encoder (E) compresses the image into a compact latent, the denoising U-Net does the iterative generative work there while a conditioning signal such as text enters through cross-attention, and only at the end does the decoder (D) expand the finished latent back to a full-resolution image. Moving the expensive denoising off the pixel grid is what made high-resolution generation affordable.
latent-diffusion.credit: Machine Vision and Learning Group, LMU Munich

latent-semantic-analysis.alt: An animated term-document matrix with words such as air, aliens, arms, defense, nuclear, space and treaty on the rows and documents d0 to d19 on the columns, each cell shaded by how often the word appears in the document. The animation reorders rows and columns so darker cells gather into blocks.
latent-semantic-analysis.caption: Latent semantic analysis starts from a term-document matrix, where each cell records how often a word appears in a document. By factorizing this matrix with singular value decomposition and keeping only the largest components, it recovers a small set of latent topics: groups of words and documents that vary together, here emerging as blocks. Words and documents then live in one low-dimensional space where similarity can be measured even without shared words.

latent-space.alt: A two-dimensional scatter plot of labeled topics where related items sit close together, such as a cluster of political candidates and a separate cluster of Super Bowl entries.
latent-space.caption: In a learned latent space, geometry encodes meaning: items the model considers similar are placed near one another. This embedding of topics groups the political candidates in one region and the football entries in another, which is why nearest-neighbor search in such a space drives semantic retrieval and clustering.

latent-variable-model.alt: A plate-notation graphical model. Hyperparameters alpha and beta sit outside two nested rectangles (plates). Inside, an arrow runs from an unshaded node theta to an unshaded node z to a single shaded node w, with beta also pointing into w. The inner plate around z and w is labeled N and the outer plate is labeled M.
latent-variable-model.caption: A latent variable model explains observed data through hidden causes, drawn here in plate notation. Only the shaded node w (an observed word) is measured; the unshaded nodes theta and z are latent variables that generate it, and the nested rectangles (plates) mean the structure repeats over N words and M documents. Inference is the task of recovering the hidden nodes from the one shaded node.

learning-rate.alt: Gradient descent on a parabola with a learning rate that is too large: successive steps overshoot the minimum and zig-zag across it with growing amplitude, diverging.
learning-rate.caption: The learning rate is the step size. Set it too large and each step overshoots the bottom of the valley; the iterates bounce across the minimum with growing amplitude and diverge instead of converging, which is why the learning rate is the single most important hyperparameter to tune.
learning-rate.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

learning-rate-schedule.alt: Plot of learning rate versus epoch following a cosine curve, starting high near 0.3 and decaying smoothly toward zero by the end of training.
learning-rate-schedule.caption: A learning-rate schedule changes the step size over training rather than holding it fixed. This cosine-annealed schedule starts large for fast early progress, then decays smoothly toward zero so the optimizer can settle precisely into a minimum at the end.
learning-rate-schedule.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

learning-rate-warmup.alt: Plot of learning rate versus epoch that ramps linearly up from zero to a peak over the first few epochs, then decays along a cosine curve toward zero.
learning-rate-warmup.caption: Warmup begins with a near-zero learning rate and ramps it up over the first epochs before the main decay takes over. The gentle start keeps the volatile first steps small and lets an adaptive optimizer's running statistics accumulate, stabilizing the most fragile phase of training.
learning-rate-warmup.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

lie-group.alt: A unit circle in the plane with the point 1 marked on the right at the origin's level, and three points z, w, and zw on the upper arc.
lie-group.caption: The circle is the simplest Lie group: a group that is also a smooth manifold. Its points are unit-length complex numbers, the group operation multiplies them (which adds their angles, so z times w lands at zw), the identity is the point 1, and every element has an inverse. Because the points vary smoothly, you can do calculus on the symmetry itself, which is what makes continuous symmetry tractable.

linear-regression.alt: Scatter plot of blue data points with a red straight line of best fit drawn through them.
linear-regression.caption: Linear regression fits a straight line through the data by choosing the slope and intercept that minimize the squared vertical gaps between the line and the points. The result is the line that sits as close as possible to all the data, the simplest model of how the output changes with the input.

linear-transformation.alt: A unit square at the origin sheared into a slanted parallelogram. The base stays fixed along the x-axis while the top edge slides sideways, so the point P moves horizontally to P prime.
linear-transformation.caption: A linear transformation keeps the origin fixed and sends straight lines to straight lines, keeping parallel lines parallel and evenly spaced. This shear illustrates that: the square becomes a parallelogram, points slide along the horizontal but no line is ever bent. Every such map is represented by a matrix, which is why each layer of a network is a linear transformation followed by a nonlinearity.

local-attractor-basin.alt: Diagram of a region A inside a larger space, with trajectories starting from several different points all flowing inward and converging into region A.
local-attractor-basin.caption: A basin of attraction is the catchment area of a minimum: every starting point inside it flows downhill to the same attractor (region A). Which solution training reaches is decided by which basin the initial parameters fall into, and wide, flat basins tend to generalize better than narrow ones.

local-minimum.alt: A one-dimensional curve with arrows labeling its global maximum, a local maximum, a local minimum, and the global minimum.
local-minimum.caption: A local minimum is lower than every nearby point, so the gradient vanishes and a downhill optimizer comes to rest there, but it need not be the global minimum, the lowest point overall. The danger in classical optimization is settling into a local minimum that is not the deepest one.

logistic-regression.alt: The logistic sigmoid curve, an S-shaped curve that rises smoothly from zero at the far left through one half at the center to one at the far right.
logistic-regression.caption: Logistic regression passes a weighted sum of the features through this S-shaped sigmoid, which squashes any real number into the range zero to one. The output is read as the probability of the positive class: large positive scores approach one, large negative scores approach zero, and a score of zero maps to one half.

logits.alt: A network takes an input image and emits three raw scores for the classes cat, dog, and horse: 35.4, 38.1, and minus 5.0. These pass through a softmax box and come out as probabilities 0.06, 0.94, and 0.00.
logits.caption: Logits are the raw, unnormalized scores a model assigns to each option, here 35.4, 38.1, and minus 5.0 for three classes. They carry no direct meaning as probabilities until a softmax exponentiates and normalizes them into a distribution that sums to one (0.06, 0.94, 0.00). Every sampling control, temperature, top-p, and a repetition penalty, edits these logits before the softmax runs.

lora.alt: An input vector x of dimension d feeds two parallel paths whose outputs are added to give h. The left path is a large frozen square weight matrix W of size d by d. The right path is a low-rank pair: a down-projection A initialized from a normal distribution narrowing to rank r, then an up-projection B initialized to zero.
lora.caption: LoRA freezes the large pretrained weight matrix W and learns only a small low-rank update beside it, formed as the product of two thin matrices A and B with inner rank r far smaller than the dimension d. Because B starts at zero, training begins exactly at the pretrained behavior, and only A and B receive gradients, so a huge model is adapted by training a tiny fraction of its parameters.

loss-function.alt: Plot of several classification loss functions (zero-one, hinge, log, squared hinge, modified Huber, perceptron) as a function of the decision-function output, all decreasing as the prediction becomes more confidently correct.
loss-function.caption: A loss function maps a prediction to a single penalty that training drives down. Plotting common classification losses against the model's output shows how each penalizes a wrong or unconfident prediction differently, so the choice of loss defines what the model is actually rewarded for learning.

loss-landscape.alt: Three-dimensional surface plot of the Rastrigin function: a rugged landscape covered by a regular grid of many local minima and peaks, with one deepest global minimum near the center.
loss-landscape.caption: Plotting a loss as a height over parameter space gives a landscape the optimizer must descend. A rugged surface like this, dense with local minima, ridges, and one global minimum, shows why the shape of the terrain, not just the optimizer, decides how hard training is.

loss-of-plasticity.alt: A plot of percent correct on the test set against task number over thousands of sequential tasks on Continual ImageNet; the red Backpropagation curve rises then collapses sharply downward below 82 percent, while Continual Backpropagation, L2-Regularization and Shrink and Perturb stay near 89 to 90 percent.
loss-of-plasticity.caption: Under sustained continual training, a standard backpropagation network (red) loses its ability to keep learning and its accuracy collapses, even though its capacity is unchanged; methods that inject fresh variability or regularize, such as continual backpropagation, retain plasticity and stay high.

lottery-ticket-hypothesis.alt: Test accuracy curves over training iterations for a full network and for sparse subnetworks keeping 64, 16.78, 4.40 and 1.15 percent of weights, where the pruned subnetworks reset to their original initialization match the full network, while the control subnetworks with the same structure but random reinitialization train to lower accuracy, the gap widening as sparsity increases.
lottery-ticket-hypothesis.caption: A winning ticket is a sparse subnetwork that, kept at its original initialization, trains to the full network's accuracy (colored lines tracking the base), while the same structure reinitialized at random (the control lines) does notably worse, showing that the lucky starting weights, not just the connectivity, are what matters.

lstm.alt: Schematic of a long short-term memory cell showing the input, forget, and output gates and the cell state
lstm.caption: An LSTM cell carries a protected cell state across time, with input, forget, and output gates deciding what to store, discard, and emit at each step.

machine-learning.alt: A grid of small scatter plots: three rows are three datasets of red and blue points, and the columns are different classifiers, each shading the plane into colored decision regions that separate the two classes.
machine-learning.caption: Machine learning fits a model to labeled examples rather than following hand-coded rules. Here the same red and blue training points (rows are three datasets) are fit by ten different classifiers (columns); each learns a decision boundary, the colored regions, that separates the classes and is meant to generalize to new points.

machine-translation.alt: A grid aligning an English sentence on the rows ('And the programme has been implemented') with a French sentence on the columns ('Le programme a été mis en application'). A filled dot in a cell marks that the row word and column word correspond; the English word 'implemented' has three dots, aligning to 'mis', 'en' and 'application'.
machine-translation.caption: Translation is not word-for-word substitution, as this alignment between an English sentence and its French version shows. Each dot links words that correspond, and the single English word 'implemented' maps to three French words, 'mis en application'. Capturing such one-to-many and reordered alignments is exactly what the attention mechanism gave neural translation.

mamba.alt: Diagram of a selective state space model: a hidden state h flows from left (h at t minus 1) to right (h at t) through the state matrix A; the current input x at t is passed through a Project block (the selection mechanism) to produce the input-dependent matrices B, C, and the step size delta, which are discretized and combined to update the state and emit output y, with a side note showing fast GPU SRAM versus slower GPU HBM memory.
mamba.caption: Mamba makes the state space recurrence selective: the input x is projected to produce the matrices B and C and the step size delta as functions of the current token, so the model decides token by token what to write into its hidden state and what to discard. Because this input-dependent recurrence can no longer be run as a fixed convolution, Mamba uses a hardware-aware scan that keeps the large state in fast GPU SRAM rather than slower HBM.

manifold.alt: A sphere with a small patch on top mapped upward by projection lines onto a flat disk, representing a coordinate chart from a region of the sphere to flat space.
manifold.caption: The defining property of a manifold is that every small region maps smoothly onto a patch of flat space. Here a neighborhood of the sphere is carried by a coordinate chart onto a flat disk, so the curved surface looks ordinary and Euclidean when you zoom in on any one piece.

manifold-optimization.alt: A sphere with a flat plane touching it at a single point, marked by a dot, representing the tangent plane to the surface at that point.
manifold-optimization.caption: Manifold optimization moves through the tangent space rather than off the surface. At each point of a curved constraint set (here a sphere) the tangent plane holds the directions a step is actually allowed to take; the Riemannian gradient is the ordinary gradient projected onto this plane, and the optimizer then steps along the manifold so every iterate stays on the valid set instead of being projected back after the fact.

markov-chain.alt: State diagram of a two-state Markov chain with states E and A; arrows show E to E at 0.3, E to A at 0.7, A to E at 0.4, and A to A at 0.6.
markov-chain.caption: A Markov chain moves between states with the next state depending only on the present one. Each state's outgoing transition probabilities sum to one (0.3 plus 0.7 from E, 0.4 plus 0.6 from A), the rows of its transition matrix.

markov-chain-monte-carlo.alt: Six small plots arranged in a grid, labeled 100, 500, 1000, 5000, 10000, and 50000 samples. Each overlays a blue target density (a bimodal curve with a tall left peak and a smaller right bump) with an orange curve estimated from the samples. The orange curve is jagged and off at 100 samples and grows steadily closer to the blue curve until the two are nearly identical at 50000 samples.
markov-chain-monte-carlo.caption: Markov chain Monte Carlo builds a random walk whose long-run equilibrium is the target distribution. With few samples the estimate (orange) is rough and misses the target (blue), but as the chain runs longer the collected samples reproduce the target ever more closely. This convergence in the limit is what lets MCMC approximate posteriors that have no closed form.

markov-decision-process.alt: A directed graph with three green state nodes (S0, S1, S2) and orange action nodes (a0, a1) branching from each state. Edges from action nodes carry transition probabilities such as 0.7 and 0.3, and two yellow squiggly arrows mark rewards of plus 5 and minus 1.
markov-decision-process.caption: A Markov decision process is defined by states (green), the actions available in each state (orange), the probabilities of where each action leads (the numbers on the edges, such as 0.7 and 0.3), and the rewards it pays (the yellow plus 5 and minus 1 arrows). The next state depends only on the current state and action, which is the Markov property that makes the problem solvable.

masked-autoencoder.alt: Masked autoencoder pipeline: an input image with most patches hidden, a heavy encoder that processes only the visible patches, and a lightweight decoder that fills in placeholder tokens to reconstruct the full target image.
masked-autoencoder.caption: A masked autoencoder learns by filling in the blanks. Most patches are hidden, a heavy encoder processes only the small visible set, and a lightweight decoder reconstructs the missing pixels. Because the target is the image's own content, no labels are needed, and the high masking ratio forces genuine understanding rather than copying nearby pixels.
masked-autoencoder.credit: Kaiming He, Xinlei Chen, Saining Xie, Yanghao Li, Piotr Dollar, Ross Girshick

matrix.alt: An m-by-n matrix drawn in large brackets with entries a sub i,j. A blue arrow down the left marks the m rows where the index i changes, and a red arrow across the top marks the n columns where the index j changes.
matrix.caption: A matrix is a rectangular grid of numbers with m rows and n columns, each entry addressed by its row index i and column index j. This two-index layout is what lets one matrix hold a whole table of data, and, read as a function, apply a linear transformation to every vector at once.

matrix-multiplication.alt: Matrix A on the left and matrix B above feed into the product matrix at lower right. A highlighted row of A and a highlighted column of B both point with colored arrows to a single highlighted entry of the result.
matrix-multiplication.caption: Each entry of the product is the dot product of one row of the left matrix with one column of the right matrix, as the colored arrows show. The shared inner dimension must match for these row-by-column dot products to line up, which is the single rule that governs whether two matrices can be multiplied.

maximum-likelihood-estimation.alt: Curve of the likelihood of observed data as a function of a Poisson rate parameter, rising to a single peak at the value marked lambda-hat and falling away on either side.
maximum-likelihood-estimation.caption: Maximum likelihood estimation picks the parameter value that makes the observed data most probable. The likelihood is read as a function of the parameter, and the estimate is the value at its peak, here the rate lambda-hat.

measure-theory.alt: Two panels showing the area under the same hump-shaped curve: the top panel approximates it with tall vertical strips, the bottom panel approximates it with stacked horizontal bands.
measure-theory.caption: The two ways to measure the area under a curve. Riemann integration (top) slices the domain into vertical strips, while Lebesgue integration (bottom), the integral built on measure theory, slices the range into horizontal bands and weights each by the measure of the set of inputs that reach it. Slicing the range is what makes the Lebesgue integral handle wildly discontinuous functions and interchange cleanly with limits.

message-passing.alt: A central node x0 connected to four neighbor nodes x1, x2, x3, x4; each neighbor's feature passes through a message function psi, and the four messages flow inward to update x0.
message-passing.caption: One round of message passing: the central node x0 gathers a message from each neighbor, every message computed by the same function psi, then aggregates them with a permutation-invariant operator to update its own state. Stacking k such rounds lets a node's representation absorb everything within k hops.

metric-tensor.alt: A globe in orthographic view overlaid with a regular grid of circles that stay round near the center and flatten into ellipses toward the rim.
metric-tensor.caption: The metric tensor is the local rule for measuring length and angle, and it can change from place to place. A grid of identical circles on the sphere appears round at the center but squashes into ellipses toward the edge, showing how the same true distance is stretched differently depending on position and direction.

metropolis-hastings.alt: Two side-by-side plots, each showing a solid target density curve P*(x) and a dashed proposal density curve Q centered on a marked current point. On the left the proposal is centered at x superscript 1 in a high-density region; on the right it is centered at x superscript 2 out in a low-density tail.
metropolis-hastings.caption: Metropolis-Hastings samples a target density P*(x) (solid) that it can only evaluate up to a constant. From the current point it draws a candidate from a proposal distribution Q (dashed) centered there, then accepts or rejects using the ratio of target densities at the two points. Because that ratio cancels the unknown normalizing constant, only relative densities are ever needed.

min-p-sampling.alt: A bar chart of next-token probabilities with one tall dominant bar on the left and a long low tail. A horizontal line marks the min-p threshold at 0.1, set as a fraction of the tallest bar; tokens below the line are shaded out and annotated as low-probability tokens filtered from sampling, increasing coherence.
min-p-sampling.caption: Min-p sets its cutoff relative to the most likely token: with min-p 0.1, any token at least one tenth as probable as the top one is kept, and the rest are dropped. The threshold therefore moves with the model's confidence. When one token dominates, as here, almost nothing else clears the relative bar, so generation stays focused even at high temperature.

mind-body-problem.alt: Four small diagrams contrasting positions on mind-body causation, each using orange mental nodes and blue physical nodes joined by arrows: interactionist dualism, epiphenomenalism, psychophysical parallelism, and non-reductive physicalism.
mind-body-problem.caption: The mind-body problem is the question of how mental states (orange) relate to physical states (blue). The positions differ over which arrows are real: interactionist dualism has mind and body cause each other, epiphenomenalism lets the physical produce the mental but denies the mental any effect, parallelism has the two run in step without interacting, and non-reductive physicalism grounds the mental in the physical while granting it downward influence.
mind-body-problem.credit: Davidl on de.wikipedia. Updated by richardbrucebaxter. Converted to SVG by Bea

mirror-neuron.alt: A flowchart. Perception of an action leads to goal attribution, asking whether the action has a goal. If yes, one route is mirror neurons performing internal imitation (embodied simulation); if no, the action is handled by classical inference.
mirror-neuron.caption: This figure illustrates the simulation account of mirror neurons. When an observed action is read as goal-directed, the brain is thought to understand it by internally re-enacting it on its own motor circuitry (embodied simulation) rather than by abstract inference alone, the same mechanism an observer would use to perform the action itself.

mixed-precision-training.alt: The bit layout of the 16-bit half-precision floating-point format: 1 sign bit, then 5 exponent bits, then 10 fraction bits, labeled from bit 15 down to bit 0.
mixed-precision-training.caption: Mixed precision training does most of its arithmetic in a low-precision 16-bit float, whose layout is shown here: one sign bit, five exponent bits, and only ten fraction bits. With half the bits of a 32-bit number, each value takes half the memory and bandwidth and runs faster on matrix units, but its narrow range and coarse precision are why the technique keeps a full-precision master copy of the weights and applies loss scaling.

mixture-of-experts.alt: The upper block diagrams a mixture-of-experts layer from DeepSeek-V2: an input hidden state feeds a router that scores many parallel expert subnetworks and keeps only the top few (Top-K), shown as a small set of activated routed experts plus shared experts, whose weighted outputs are summed into the output hidden state. The lower block shows the separate multi-head latent attention module.
mixture-of-experts.caption: A mixture-of-experts layer (upper block) replaces one dense layer with many parallel expert subnetworks and a router. For each token the router scores the experts and activates only the top few, so total parameters can grow large while the compute spent per token stays small. A load-balancing pressure keeps the router from sending every token to the same few experts. The lower block is a separate attention module, not part of the mixture-of-experts idea.

mode-connectivity.alt: A two-dimensional loss contour with two trained network minima marked at the lower left and lower right; a solid two-segment path with one bend connects them through the low-loss red region, while a dashed straight line between them dips into a blue high-loss valley.
mode-connectivity.caption: Two independently trained minima are not isolated valleys: the dashed straight line between them crosses high loss, but the solid bent path stays entirely in the low-loss region, the low-loss tunnel that mode connectivity says links distinct solutions.

model-context-protocol.alt: Two node diagrams compared. On the left, eight nodes labeled A to H are each wired directly to every other node, forming a dense tangle of connections, labeled Impact equals N. On the right, the same eight nodes each connect only to a single central hub, labeled Impact equals 1.
model-context-protocol.caption: MCP applies the standard-hub pattern shown here to the boundary between models and tools. Without a shared standard, connecting many applications to many tools means a separate custom connector for nearly every pair, a tangle that grows as M times N (left). Routing everything through one common protocol collapses that to M plus N (right): a tool implements the standard once and any compliant client can use it.
model-context-protocol.credit: ESB_versus_POINT_2_POINT.gif : Original uploader was Arkramer at nl.wikipedia de

model-parallelism.alt: Four schematics of a convolutional network distributed across GPUs. From left to right: the original undivided problem; network partitioning, where whole layers are assigned to different GPUs; layer-wise partitioning, where each layer is split across two GPUs with activations exchanged between them; and data parallelism, where each GPU holds the full network.
model-parallelism.caption: Model parallelism splits a single network across devices rather than replicating it. The two middle panels show its two flavors: network partitioning puts whole layers on different GPUs (the basis of pipeline parallelism), and layer-wise partitioning splits each individual layer across GPUs that exchange activations (the basis of tensor parallelism). Both differ from data parallelism on the right, where the whole network is copied to each device.
model-parallelism.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

momentum.alt: Contour plot of an elongated, ill-conditioned valley with a momentum optimizer path that overshoots once then spirals inward, settling at the minimum.
momentum.caption: In a long narrow valley, momentum accumulates a velocity from past gradients: the across-valley components of successive steps cancel while the along-valley components add up, so the path damps its oscillation and travels efficiently toward the minimum instead of zig-zagging.
momentum.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

monte-carlo-tree-search.alt: Four copies of the same search tree labeled Selection, Expansion, Simulation, and Backpropagation. Each node shows a wins-over-visits ratio such as 11/21. Selection traces a path down to a leaf, Expansion adds a new child, Simulation plays out from it, and Backpropagation updates the ratios back up the path.
monte-carlo-tree-search.caption: Monte Carlo tree search repeats four steps to decide a move. Selection walks down the tree by a rule that favors high-value, rarely-tried nodes; Expansion adds a new node at the frontier; Simulation plays out a result from there; and Backpropagation sends that result back up, updating each node's wins-over-visits count. Thousands of these passes concentrate effort on the most promising lines.

multi-head-attention.alt: Diagram of multi-head attention: queries, keys, and values each pass through separate fully connected layers for each of several heads, each head runs its own attention, the head outputs are concatenated, and a final fully connected layer combines them.
multi-head-attention.caption: Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel. The queries, keys, and values are projected separately for each head by its own linear layer, each head performs scaled dot-product attention in its own subspace, and the head outputs are concatenated and mixed by a final linear layer. Splitting the model width across heads lets different heads capture different relationships at about the cost of one full-width attention.
multi-head-attention.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

multi-query-attention.alt: Three attention layouts compared. Multi-head on the left gives every query column its own key and value. Grouped-query in the middle lets each group of query columns share one key and one value. Multi-query on the right has all query columns share a single key and value.
multi-query-attention.caption: Multi-query attention (right) takes key and value sharing to its limit: all query heads read from a single key and value head, shrinking the KV cache to its minimum for fast inference. Compared with multi-head attention (left), this can cost quality, which grouped-query attention (middle) later softened by sharing within groups rather than across all heads.
multi-query-attention.credit: Joshua Ainslie et al. (GQA paper, arXiv:2305.13245)

multilayer-perceptron.alt: Diagram of a multilayer perceptron with an input layer of four units, a fully connected hidden layer of five units, and an output layer of three units, every unit in a layer joined to every unit in the next.
multilayer-perceptron.caption: A multilayer perceptron stacks fully connected layers: each unit in the hidden layer takes a weighted sum of every input and passes it through a nonlinear activation, and the output layer reads from every hidden unit. This dense layer-to-layer connection, with a nonlinearity between layers, is what lets the network approximate complex functions.
multilayer-perceptron.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

mutual-information.alt: Venn diagram of two overlapping circles for the entropies of X and Y, with the overlap labeled mutual information and the non-overlapping parts labeled the conditional entropies.
mutual-information.caption: Mutual information is the overlap between two variables' entropies: the uncertainty about one that is removed by learning the other. It equals the entropy of X minus the entropy of X given Y, and it is zero exactly when the variables are independent.

needle-in-a-haystack.alt: A heatmap with context length in tokens on the horizontal axis and the depth at which a fact is placed in the document on the vertical axis; cells are green where the model retrieved the fact and red where it failed, with more red appearing at longer context lengths.
needle-in-a-haystack.caption: The needle-in-a-haystack test hides one fact at many depths inside documents of many lengths and asks the model to retrieve it, coloring each cell green for success and red for failure. The growing band of red at longer contexts shows that a model's effective context length can fall well short of its advertised window.

neural-coding.alt: A plot of several overlapping bell-shaped tuning curves of different colors, each peaking at a different position along the stimulus axis, so any given stimulus value activates several neurons by differing amounts.
neural-coding.caption: A population code is one answer to how neurons represent information. Each neuron responds most strongly to its own preferred stimulus value, with a broad bell-shaped tuning curve, so a single stimulus lights up many neurons by differing amounts. The value can then be read out far more accurately from the whole population than from any one cell, and the code degrades gracefully when neurons are noisy.

neural-network.alt: Diagram of a feedforward neural network with an input layer, a hidden layer, and an output layer of connected nodes
neural-network.caption: A feedforward network passes inputs through layers of weighted, nonlinear units; depth lets later layers compose features discovered by earlier ones.

neural-scaling-laws.alt: A log-axis plot of optimal tokens per parameter against training compute in FLOP, with fitted lines for two estimates of the compute-optimal policy and a dashed horizontal line marking the rule of thumb of about 20 tokens per parameter, with the Chinchilla model plotted on it.
neural-scaling-laws.caption: Scaling laws not only predict loss from compute but also set how to spend a budget: the Chinchilla result found that for compute-optimal training the number of parameters and the number of training tokens should grow in roughly equal proportion, near 20 tokens per parameter (dashed line).

neural-tangent-kernel.alt: Plot titled NTK describes NN evolution, showing a spread of pink curves for an ensemble of neural networks and a single blue line for the NTK mean estimator fitting scattered data points.
neural-tangent-kernel.caption: In the infinite-width limit, the neural tangent kernel predicts training as kernel regression with a fixed kernel: the blue NTK mean estimator tracks the average of an ensemble of wide networks (pink), so a nonlinear network behaves like a linear model in a frozen feature space.

neuromorphic-computing.alt: Diagram of a neuromorphic chip's mesh routing. A source neuron X in one core sends a spike that is routed by axon identifiers across a grid of separate cores, reaching target neurons A through F located on different cores.
neuromorphic-computing.caption: Neuromorphic hardware colocates many neurons and synapses across a grid of cores and communicates with sparse, asynchronous spikes rather than a global clock. Here one neuron's spike is routed by address to its targets on several cores at once, so memory and computation sit together and work happens only when a spike occurs, the source of the design's low power use. The figure is from Intel's Loihi.

newtons-method.alt: Contour plot of an elongated bowl with two paths from a start point x0 to the minimum x: a red path that takes many short zig-zag steps and a green path that reaches the minimum in a few large curved steps.
newtons-method.caption: Both methods start at x0 and aim for the minimum x, but they use different information. Gradient descent (red) sees only the slope and crawls down the stretched valley in many small zig-zag steps. Newton's method (green) also uses the curvature to build a local quadratic model and jumps almost straight to the bottom in a handful of steps.

next-token-prediction.alt: A sequence model unrolled over six time steps. The input sequence reads m, a, c, h, i, n along the bottom, each feeding a hidden layer that passes state to the next step and an output layer above. The target sequence at the top is a, c, h, i, n, e, the input shifted left by one character.
next-token-prediction.caption: Next-token prediction trains a model to predict the following symbol at every position, so the target sequence is the input shifted by one (here the input machin against the target achine). At each step the model outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary for the next token given everything before it; generation then samples one token at a time. Transformers are trained on the same objective.
next-token-prediction.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

no-free-lunch-theorem.alt: A bar chart titled Maximization Speed of Algorithms A and B. For each of eight objective functions along the horizontal axis, two bars show the number of evaluations each algorithm needs. Algorithm A is faster on some functions and algorithm B on others, with neither dominating across all of them.
no-free-lunch-theorem.caption: Across a set of objective functions, algorithm A needs fewer evaluations on some and algorithm B on others, and the advantages cancel when summed over all of them. This is the No Free Lunch theorem in miniature: averaged over every possible problem, no algorithm beats any other, so any real-world edge comes from assumptions matched to the task, not from a universally best method.

node-embedding.alt: Left, a graph with a biased random walk highlighted; right, the same nodes placed in a two-dimensional embedding space where they form two clusters, one red and one blue, with a single bridge node between them.
node-embedding.caption: A node embedding maps each node of a graph to a coordinate in a continuous space so that graph-proximate nodes land close together. Here biased random walks over the graph (left) are turned into vectors (right), where the two communities separate into distinct clusters and the bridging node sits between them; closeness in the space now means closeness in the graph.

norm.alt: Nested closed curves in the plane, all passing through the points one unit along each axis: a diamond for p equal to 1, a circle for p equal to 2, rounded squares for p equal to 3 and 6, and a square for p infinity.
norm.caption: Each curve is the unit ball of a different norm: the set of vectors of length one. The L1 norm gives a diamond, the familiar Euclidean L2 norm a circle, and the L-infinity norm a square. The shape matters in practice, which is why an L1 penalty drives weights exactly to zero (sparsity) while an L2 penalty shrinks them smoothly.

normalizing-flow.alt: A chain of nodes z0 through zk linked by invertible functions f1 to fk, with a probability density drawn beneath each node: under z0 a single smooth Gaussian bump, growing through the chain into a complex multi-peaked distribution under zk, which equals the data x.
normalizing-flow.caption: A normalizing flow turns a simple distribution into a complex one through a chain of exactly invertible functions. Samples start as an easy Gaussian (z0, a single smooth bump) and each invertible step bends the density further, until the final variable zk matches the multi-peaked shape of the real data. Because every function is invertible, the same chain can be run backward to read off the exact likelihood of any data point.

object-detection.alt: Two object-detection pipelines compared: (a) a two-stage detector that generates region proposals before classifying them, and (b) a one-stage detector that predicts boxes directly, both ending in classification (cls) and localization (loc) outputs.
object-detection.caption: Object detection is recognition plus localization: every output carries both a class label (cls) and a box location (loc). Two-stage detectors (a) first propose candidate regions and then classify each, while one-stage detectors (b) predict boxes and labels directly in a single pass, trading some accuracy for speed.
object-detection.credit: Licheng Jiao, Fan Zhang, Fang Liu, Shuyuan Yang

occams-razor.alt: A scatter of red and blue dots scattered around a smooth black curve. A green boundary wiggles back and forth to enclose each individual point of one class, separating the two colors perfectly but following a far more complicated path than the smooth black curve.
occams-razor.caption: Two boundaries separate the same data. The smooth black curve captures the simple underlying trend, while the green curve contorts itself to classify every training point correctly, including the noise. Occam's Razor prefers the simpler explanation: the smooth boundary is more likely to generalize, and the complicated one is probably fitting accidents of this particular sample.

optical-flow.alt: A red blob shown at five successive positions numbered 1 to 5 across a black background, with a white arrow tracing its displacement from the first position to the last.
optical-flow.caption: Optical flow is the apparent motion between frames, expressed as a displacement vector for each point. Tracking one feature across successive frames (positions 1 to 5) gives the vector (white arrow) describing how far and in which direction it moved, the per-pixel version of which is the dense flow field that drives tracking and video understanding.

optimal-transport.alt: Scatter plot with blue plus signs marking source samples on the lower left and red crosses marking target samples on the upper right, with thin lines connecting sources to targets according to the optimal transport plan.
optimal-transport.caption: Optimal transport finds the cheapest plan to move one distribution of mass onto another. Here the lines show how mass at each source point (blue) is routed to target points (red) so that the total distance moved is as small as possible; the cost of this best plan is the Wasserstein distance, a way to compare distributions that respects the geometry of the space.

orthogonality.alt: Two unit vectors drawn from a point, one labeled e parallel lying along a line and one labeled e perpendicular, with a small square marking the right angle between them.
orthogonality.caption: Two vectors are orthogonal when they meet at a right angle, equivalently when their dot product is zero, so moving along one changes nothing along the other. Unit vectors that are mutually orthogonal, like these, form an orthonormal frame, the cleanest coordinate system, and a matrix built from such columns rotates space without distorting any length or angle.

overfitting.alt: Three panels fitting polynomials of degree 1, 4, and 15 to the same noisy samples of a curved true function. Degree 1 is a straight line that misses the curve, degree 4 tracks the true function closely, and degree 15 wiggles through every point.
overfitting.caption: The same data fit by polynomials of increasing flexibility. The degree-15 model on the right overfits: it contorts itself to pass through the noise, so it scores well on these points but its wild swings would generalize poorly. The degree-4 model in the middle, which matches the true function, is the better fit despite not hitting every sample.

paged-attention.alt: A contiguous virtual address space (with text, data, and stack regions) on the left, connected by arrows to a physical address space on the right whose pages are scattered out of order, with a legend marking pages that belong to the process and pages that do not.
paged-attention.caption: Paged attention borrows this idea from operating-system virtual memory. A program sees one contiguous address space (left), but the pages are mapped to scattered, non-contiguous frames of physical memory (right) through a table of pointers. Paged attention stores a request's growing key-value cache the same way, in small fixed-size blocks handed out on demand, so memory is not reserved for the worst case and almost none is wasted.
paged-attention.credit: Traced by User:Stannered, original by en:User:Dysprosia

parallel-transport.alt: A sphere on which a vector is carried from point A along the equator to B, then up a meridian to the north pole N, then back down to A, returning rotated by an angle alpha.
parallel-transport.caption: Parallel transport carries a vector along a path while turning it only as much as staying tangent to the surface requires. Moved around the closed loop A to B to the pole and back to A, the vector returns rotated by an angle alpha; this failure to come back unchanged is exactly the curvature enclosed by the loop.

parameter-efficient-fine-tuning.alt: A Venn diagram of overlapping colored circles labeled additive, adapters, soft prompts, selective, and reparametrization-based, with parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods placed in each region: BitFit and LN Tuning under selective, Prompt-tuning and Prefix-Tuning under soft prompts, LoRA and KronA under reparametrization-based, and Adapters and Compacter in the overlaps.
parameter-efficient-fine-tuning.caption: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning is a family of methods that freeze most of a pretrained model and train only a small set of parameters. They fall into groups: additive methods that insert new modules (adapters or soft prompts such as prefix-tuning), selective methods that tune a chosen sliver of existing weights (such as BitFit), and reparametrization-based methods that add a low-rank update (LoRA). The shared bet is that the change needed to specialize a model is small and low-dimensional.
parameter-efficient-fine-tuning.credit: Vladislav Lialin, Vijeta Deshpande, Anna Rumshisky

part-of-speech-tagging.alt: The sentence 'displaCy uses JavaScript, SVG and CSS.' with a grammatical tag printed in color under each word: NN, VBZ, NNP, NNP, CC, NNP. Curved labeled arcs above the words show the dependency relations nsubj, dobj, conj and cc.
part-of-speech-tagging.caption: Part-of-speech tagging labels every word with its grammatical category, here NN for a noun, VBZ for a verb, NNP for a proper noun, and CC for a coordinating conjunction. Because a word like 'uses' could be a noun or a verb, the tag is decided from the whole sentence, which makes it a sequence task. These tags are the scaffold later steps such as parsing build on, as the arcs above suggest.

partial-derivative.alt: A three-dimensional bowl-shaped surface z over inputs x and y, sliced by a vertical plane at a fixed value of y; the intersection of the plane and the surface is drawn as a red curve.
partial-derivative.caption: A partial derivative holds every input but one fixed. Slicing the surface z = f(x, y) with a vertical plane at a constant y leaves the red cross-section, a curve in x alone, and the slope of that curve is the partial derivative of f with respect to x.

perceptron.alt: Diagram of a single artificial neuron: inputs x1 through xn each multiplied by a weight, fed into a summation node, then through an activation function with a threshold, producing one output.
perceptron.caption: A perceptron is one computing unit. It multiplies each input by a learned weight, sums the results, and passes the total through a threshold to emit a single output. Training nudges the weights toward correct answers. Every neuron in a modern network is this same primitive with a smooth activation in place of the hard threshold.

pipeline-parallelism.alt: A diagram of 3D parallelism. Two data-parallel ranks each contain four pipeline stages laid left to right, and each stage is labeled with a contiguous block of network layers (layers 0 to 7, 8 to 15, 16 to 23, 24 to 31). Within each stage the work is further split across model-parallel slices.
pipeline-parallelism.caption: Pipeline parallelism cuts a deep network between its layers: each stage holds a contiguous block of layers (here layers 0 to 7, then 8 to 15, and so on) and passes activations to the next stage like an assembly line. The figure also shows pipeline parallelism composed with data parallelism (the two ranks) and model parallelism (the slices within a stage), which is how the largest models are actually trained.

polysemanticity.alt: Three optimized feature-visualization images for a single neuron, each blending several unrelated things at once: cat faces together with the fronts of cars, and cat legs together with car bodies.
polysemanticity.caption: A polysemantic neuron responds to several unrelated concepts at once. The optimized images that most excite this one neuron mix cat faces with car fronts and cat legs with car bodies, so the neuron does not stand for any single human concept. This is why neurons are poor units of interpretation: meaning is not cleanly aligned with individual neurons.
polysemanticity.credit: Olah, Cammarata, Schubert, Goh, Petrov, Carter

pooling.alt: A 4 by 4 grid of numbers divided into four colored 2 by 2 windows, with an arrow to a 2 by 2 output holding the maximum of each window: 6, 8, 3, 4.
pooling.caption: Max pooling downsamples a feature map by sliding a window and keeping only the largest value in each region. Each colored 2 by 2 block collapses to its maximum (6, 8, 3, 4), halving width and height and granting a small amount of translation invariance because only the strongest response is kept, not its exact position.

positional-encoding.alt: Heatmap of a sinusoidal positional encoding matrix, with sequence position on the vertical axis and encoding dimension on the horizontal axis. Low dimensions on the left oscillate rapidly down the rows while higher dimensions on the right oscillate ever more slowly, giving each position a distinct multi-frequency code.
positional-encoding.caption: A sinusoidal positional encoding assigns every position a vector built from sine and cosine functions at many frequencies, here one matrix row per position. The low dimensions on the left change quickly with position while the high dimensions on the right change slowly, so the combination gives the otherwise order-blind transformer both a fine and a coarse sense of where each token sits.
positional-encoding.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

positive-definite-matrix.alt: A 3D surface plot of the function z equal to x squared over 4 plus y squared over 4, an upward-opening elliptic paraboloid that forms a single bowl with its lowest point at the origin.
positive-definite-matrix.caption: A symmetric matrix is positive definite when its quadratic form is positive for every nonzero vector, which carves out a bowl that curves upward in every direction, like this surface with one lowest point. That all-directions-upward shape is what marks a genuine minimum (and a convex function), as opposed to the saddle of an indefinite matrix, and every covariance matrix has this property.

pre-normalization.alt: Two transformer decoders side by side. On the left, norm-last, a normalization layer sits after each sublayer on the residual path. On the right, norm-first, the normalization layer is moved inside each block to the input of the sublayer, leaving the residual path from input to output free of any normalization.
pre-normalization.caption: Pre-normalization (norm-first, right) moves the layer normalization to the input of each sublayer instead of after it (norm-last, left). This leaves the residual highway running from input to output without a normalization step in the way, so gradients flow cleanly through very deep stacks, which is why pre-norm transformers are much more stable to train.

precision-and-recall.alt: Diagram of a set of items split into relevant and not relevant, with a circle marking the retrieved items, labeling the four regions true positives, false positives, false negatives, and true negatives, and showing precision and recall as fractions of those regions.
precision-and-recall.caption: Precision is the fraction of retrieved items that are relevant (true positives over everything inside the circle), while recall is the fraction of relevant items that were retrieved (true positives over the whole left band). The same true positives are divided by two different totals, which is why the two metrics trade off.

predictive-coding.alt: A two-level schematic of predictive coding. Each level has a Local Model that sends predictions P downward (blue arrows) and prediction-error units PE, drawn as triangles and circles, that send error upward (red arrows).
predictive-coding.caption: In predictive coding each cortical level holds a generative model that sends its prediction (P) down to the level below, and the level below returns only the prediction error (PE), the part of the input the prediction failed to explain. Predictions flow down and errors flow up, so what travels up the hierarchy is the mismatch, not the raw signal.

prefill-and-decode.alt: A serving diagram. Requests enter a controller that dispatches to prefill instances (each an LLM model on a parallel GPU runtime) on the left; the resulting key-value cache is transferred across to decoding instances on the right that generate the output tokens.
prefill-and-decode.caption: LLM inference splits into two phases with opposite characteristics. Prefill reads the whole prompt in one parallel pass and fills the key-value cache (compute-bound); decode then generates tokens one at a time, streaming the weights and the growing cache each step (memory-bound). The key-value cache passed between them, shown here as an explicit transfer, is the state that links the parallel prefill to the serial decode.
prefill-and-decode.credit: Yinmin Zhong, Shengyu Liu, Junda Chen, Jianbo Hu, Yibo Zhu, Xuanzhe Liu, Xin Jin, Hao Zhang

pretraining.alt: A line of text, the time machine by h g wells, shown twice: an input sequence row and a target sequence row, where the target row is the same text shifted one position to the left so each target token is the token that follows the corresponding input token.
pretraining.caption: Pretraining a language model uses a self-supervised objective that needs no human labels: the text supplies its own targets. The target sequence is just the input shifted by one position, so at every step the model predicts the next token and the true next token is the free label. Run over a vast corpus, this next-token prediction is where a base model acquires its knowledge.
pretraining.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

principal-component-analysis.alt: Scatter plot of Gaussian data with arrows marking the principal component directions
principal-component-analysis.caption: PCA finds the orthogonal directions of greatest variance; the arrows are the principal components, the new axes onto which the data is projected.
