probability-distribution.alt: Stem plot of the probability mass function of a fair six-sided die, with a height of one sixth above each outcome from 1 to 6.
probability-distribution.caption: A probability distribution assigns a probability to every outcome. For a fair die the mass function gives one sixth to each face from 1 to 6, and the six values sum to one over the whole space.

process-reward-model.alt: A tree of numbered nodes connected by colored edges, where each node is a partial chain-of-thought solution state and each edge is a reasoning step, with correct steps drawn in yellow and wrong steps in blue.
process-reward-model.caption: A process reward model scores the reasoning, not just the answer. Each node here is a partial solution and each edge is one reasoning step labeled correct (yellow) or wrong (blue), giving dense step-level feedback. This pinpoints exactly where a chain of thought goes wrong, unlike an outcome reward model that judges only the final result.
process-reward-model.credit: Liangchen Luo, Yinxiao Liu, Rosanne Liu, Samrat Phatale, Meiqi Guo, Harsh Lara, Yunxuan Li, Lei Shu, Yun Zhu, Lei Meng, Jiao Sun, Abhinav Rastogi

q-learning.alt: Two Q-tables for the Taxi task with states as rows and the six actions (South, North, East, West, Pickup, Dropoff) as columns. The top table, labeled Initialized, is all zeros; the bottom table, labeled after Training, holds learned numeric Q-values that differ across actions.
q-learning.caption: Q-learning learns a Q-table, one expected long-run value for every state-action pair. It starts at all zeros (top) and, after training, each cell holds the learned value of that action in that state (bottom). To act, the agent simply reads off the row for its current state and picks the action with the highest value.

qlora.alt: Three columns comparing finetuning methods by where memory sits and how gradients flow. Full finetuning has a 32-bit optimizer state and gradients flowing into a 16-bit base model. LoRA freezes a 16-bit base model and trains small 16-bit adapters. QLoRA freezes a 4-bit base model, trains 16-bit adapters, and adds a paging arrow that offloads optimizer state to CPU memory.
qlora.caption: QLoRA makes fine-tuning a large model fit on one GPU by stacking two ideas. The base model is quantized to 4 bits and frozen (so its weights never update and can stay compressed), small LoRA adapters carry all the learning, and paged optimizers spill optimizer state to CPU memory to absorb spikes. Weights are dequantized to higher precision on the fly for each operation, so the math stays accurate while storage is 4-bit.
qlora.credit: Tim Dettmers, Artidoro Pagnoni, Ari Holtzman, Luke Zettlemoyer

quantization.alt: A smooth grey curve overlaid with a red staircase that snaps to a small set of evenly spaced horizontal levels marked by dashed lines, so the continuous values are rounded to the nearest available level.
quantization.caption: Quantization maps continuous values onto a small set of discrete levels by rounding each to the nearest one, shown here as the red staircase tracking the smooth grey signal. A model is quantized the same way: its weights are rounded from 16-bit floats to a few integer levels (8-bit or 4-bit), which shrinks memory and speeds inference at the cost of a small rounding error.

random-forest.alt: Diagram of random forest bagging: a full dataset is randomly sampled with replacement into several smaller bootstrap datasets, each used to train a separate decision tree, whose predictions are then aggregated.
random-forest.caption: A random forest builds many trees on different random samples of the data, drawn with replacement, so no two trees see quite the same examples. Each tree predicts on its own, and the forest aggregates them by voting or averaging. Because the errors of independent trees tend to cancel, the combination is far more accurate and stable than any single tree.

recurrent-neural-network.alt: A recurrent neural network shown folded as a single cell with a self-loop, and unfolded to the right into a chain of cells across time steps t minus 1, t, and t plus 1, each taking an input x and producing an output o while passing the hidden state h forward.
recurrent-neural-network.caption: A recurrent network applies the same cell at every step, feeding its hidden state h forward in time. Unfolding the self-loop (left) into a chain (right) shows how context carries across the sequence: the hidden state at each step depends on the current input and the previous state, with the weights U, V, and W shared at every step.

regression.alt: Scatter plot of airfare cost against distance to destination, with a fitted straight line, the line equation 0.117 times x plus 83.267, and an R-squared of 0.632 noted.
regression.caption: Regression predicts a continuous quantity from input features. Here airfare cost is modeled as a line in the distance, fare equals 0.117 times distance plus 83.267, so each extra mile adds about twelve cents. The R-squared of 0.632 reports the fraction of the variation in fare the line explains, the standard way to score a continuous prediction.

regularization.alt: Several data points fit two ways: a blue curve that oscillates wildly to pass exactly through every point, and a smooth green curve that follows the general trend without hitting each point.
regularization.caption: Without regularization a flexible model can twist to pass through every point (blue), fitting the noise and generalizing badly. Regularization penalizes that complexity, favoring the smooth green curve that captures the underlying trend instead. It trades a little fit on the training points for a model far more likely to hold up on new data.

reinforcement-learning.alt: Diagram of the reinforcement learning loop showing an agent taking actions and receiving state and reward from the environment
reinforcement-learning.caption: An agent acts on its environment and receives back the next state and a reward; learning means choosing actions that maximize cumulative reward.

rejection-sampling.alt: A target density curve f of X sits below a dashed envelope curve M times g of X. The region under f is labeled the acceptance zone and the gap between f and the envelope is labeled the rejection zone; a vertical draw at point X with a uniform value U decides whether the candidate is accepted or rejected.
rejection-sampling.caption: Rejection sampling proposes candidates and keeps only those that pass a check, discarding the rest: a draw that falls in the acceptance zone (under the target) is kept, one in the rejection zone is thrown away. In language models the same generate-then-filter idea uses a verifier (a unit test, a grader) as the acceptance test, both to enforce a requirement at inference and to keep only verified-correct outputs for training.

relu.alt: Plot over inputs from minus four to four: the rectifier in blue is flat at zero for negative inputs and a straight line equal to the input for positive inputs, with the smooth softplus curve in green approximating it.
relu.caption: The rectified linear unit (blue) outputs zero for negative inputs and the input itself for positive ones, the maximum of zero and the input. Its derivative is exactly one on the positive side, so gradients pass through undiminished, unlike saturating activations; the green softplus is a smooth approximation. The sharp threshold also makes roughly half the units output zero, giving sparse, cheap activations.

reproducing-kernel-hilbert-space.alt: Two panels joined by an arrow labeled phi. The left panel shows two classes of points separated by a curved boundary in the input plane; the right panel shows the same points after a feature map, now separated by a straight line.
reproducing-kernel-hilbert-space.caption: A reproducing kernel Hilbert space is what makes the kernel trick rigorous. A feature map phi lifts the inputs into a richer (often infinite-dimensional) space where a curved separating boundary in the original input (left) becomes a simple straight one (right). A kernel computes inner products in that feature space directly, so the lift never has to be formed explicitly, and the representer theorem keeps the resulting problem finite.

residual-connection.alt: Two network blocks side by side: a regular block on the left whose stacked layers must learn the full mapping f(x), and a residual block on the right where a skip arrow adds the input x back to the block output, so the layers learn g(x) and the block computes f(x) equals g(x) plus x.
residual-connection.caption: In a residual block the input is added back to the output through a skip connection, so the stacked layers only need to learn the residual correction g(x) rather than the full mapping. The identity shortcut also gives gradients a clean path backward, which is what makes very deep networks trainable.
residual-connection.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

retrieval-augmented-generation.alt: Flow diagram of a RAG pipeline. Reference documents are passed through an LLM embedding step into a vector database. A user query is embedded into a query vector, compared against the database to find relevant documents, and the retrieved context chunks plus the original query form an augmented query, which an LLM generation step turns into the response.
retrieval-augmented-generation.caption: Retrieval-augmented generation runs in two stages. Reference documents are embedded into a vector database ahead of time; at query time the user's question is embedded, the nearest passages are retrieved, and those context chunks are concatenated with the question into an augmented prompt that the model answers from. The knowledge lives in the editable corpus, while the model supplies the reasoning and prose.

reward-model.alt: Flow diagram of reinforcement learning from human feedback: human annotators rank sampled responses into ranking data that trains a reward model, which then guides training of the aligned model through PPO, with a supervised model initializing both.
reward-model.caption: A reward model is a learned stand-in for human judgment. Human annotators rank model responses, those comparisons train the reward model to score any output, and the policy is then optimized to produce outputs the reward model rates highly. This is how the slow, expensive signal of human preference is compiled into a fast automatic reward for reinforcement learning from human feedback.

riemannian-manifold.alt: A torus, a donut-shaped surface, with a single curved red path winding around it once through the hole and around the tube.
riemannian-manifold.caption: A Riemannian manifold is a curved surface equipped with a metric that defines distance, and the shortest path between two points is a geodesic. On this torus the red geodesic curves through the hole and around the tube, the straightest available route given the surface's geometry.

rlaif.alt: Two parallel pipelines from one SFT model that samples responses. In the top path, an off-the-shelf large language model rates the response pairs, training a reward model from AI feedback that drives reinforcement learning to an RLAIF policy. In the bottom path, a human rates the pairs, training a reward model from human feedback that drives reinforcement learning to an RLHF policy.
rlaif.caption: Reinforcement learning from AI feedback keeps the RLHF machinery but swaps where the preference labels come from. Instead of a human comparing response pairs (bottom), a capable model is the judge (top), labeling preferences that train the reward model. AI feedback removes the human annotation bottleneck, applying the same criteria cheaply and at scale, and is the mechanism underneath constitutional AI.
rlaif.credit: Harrison Lee, Samrat Phatale, Hassan Mansoor, Thomas Mesnard, et al.

rlhf.alt: A flow diagram with four boxes. Prompt data trains a supervised model. Human annotators rank sampled responses to produce ranking data, which trains a reward model. The supervised model initializes both the reward model and an aligned model, and the reward model guides the aligned model through PPO reinforcement learning.
rlhf.caption: Reinforcement learning from human feedback aligns a model in three stages. A supervised model is trained on demonstrations; humans then rank pairs of its responses, and those comparisons train a reward model that predicts a scalar score. Finally the model is optimized by reinforcement learning (PPO) to maximize that reward, with a penalty keeping it close to the supervised starting point so it does not degenerate.

rmsprop.alt: Contour plot of an ill-conditioned objective with the RMSProp optimizer path following a smooth curve from the start point to the minimum.
rmsprop.caption: RMSProp divides each parameter's step by a running root-mean-square of its recent gradients, so steep and flat directions are rescaled to comparable effective step sizes. The result is a smooth, confident path to the minimum where a single shared learning rate would zig-zag.
rmsprop.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

roc-auc.alt: Top: two overlapping class score distributions with a moving threshold, shading the resulting true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives. Bottom: the ROC curve of true positive rate against false positive rate with a diagonal no-skill line and an operating point that moves as the threshold changes.
roc-auc.caption: Sliding the decision threshold across the two class score distributions changes the true positive and false positive rates, and plotting that pair at every threshold traces the ROC curve. The area under it (AUC) measures how well the model ranks positives above negatives; the dashed diagonal is chance and the top-left corner is perfect separation.

roofline-model.alt: A roofline plot with attainable performance in GFLOPS on the vertical axis and operational intensity in FLOPS per byte on the horizontal axis. A slanted line rising from the left is labeled bound based on bandwidth, a horizontal line is labeled bound based on peak performance, and the two meet at a ridge point. Three applications are marked below the roof.
roofline-model.caption: The roofline model plots attainable performance against arithmetic intensity (operations performed per byte moved). The slanted segment is the memory-bandwidth ceiling and the flat segment is the peak-compute ceiling; where they meet is the ridge point. An operation to the left of the ridge is memory-bound and speeds up only by moving less data, while one to the right is compute-bound, which is how the plot tells you which limit you are fighting.

rwkv.alt: Architecture diagram of one RWKV block: an input embedding passes up through a Layer Norm into a Time Mix block and then another Layer Norm into a Channel Mix block, with token-shift operations feeding each, and a final Layer Norm and output embedding; the time-mix block maintains a running weighted sum gated by a receptance term, and the channel-mix block plays the role of the feed-forward layer.
rwkv.caption: RWKV replaces self-attention with two interacting sub-blocks stacked in each layer. The Time Mix block plays the role of attention but, instead of comparing all pairs of tokens, maintains a running exponentially weighted sum of past key and value signals gated by a receptance term; the Channel Mix block plays the role of the feed-forward layer. Because the time decay lets the past be carried in a single fixed-size state, the same model has an equivalent recurrent form that generates in constant memory and a parallel form that trains efficiently.

saddle-point.alt: Three-dimensional surface shaped like a saddle, marked at the central point where it curves upward along one axis and downward along the other.
saddle-point.caption: At a saddle point the gradient is zero, yet the surface curves up in some directions and down in others, so it is neither a peak nor a valley. Plain gradient descent stalls here because the slope vanishes, and in high-dimensional networks such saddle points, not bad local minima, are the dominant obstacle.

sample-complexity.alt: A learning curve plotting score against the number of training examples. A red training-score curve starts high and drifts down, while a green cross-validation-score curve starts low and rises, the two converging toward each other as the number of examples grows, with shaded confidence bands around each.
sample-complexity.caption: A learning curve shows how performance depends on the amount of data. With few examples the training score is high but the cross-validation score is low, a large generalization gap. As more examples are added the two curves converge and the validation score climbs. Sample complexity is the number of examples needed to close that gap and reach a target accuracy.

scaling-laws.alt: Plot of the optimal ratio of training tokens per model parameter against training compute in FLOP on logarithmic axes. Two estimated optimal-policy curves are shown with uncertainty bands, along with a dashed horizontal line marking the D over N equals 20 rule of thumb and a point for the Chinchilla model.
scaling-laws.caption: Scaling laws let practitioners choose how to spend a fixed compute budget. The Chinchilla analysis found that parameters and training tokens should grow together, roughly twenty tokens per parameter (the dashed line), rather than making the model as large as possible. This compute-optimal allocation is why newer models are often not the largest but are trained on far more tokens.

self-attention.alt: Diagram of one self-attention head. Panel B shows each token vector T multiplied by weight matrices W-Q, W-K, W-V to make a query, key, and value. Panel A shows token 1's query compared against the keys of all four tokens, each score scaled, passed through softmax, and used to weight the value vectors into a new representation. Panel C writes the result as softmax of (Q times K-transpose over the square root of d-k) times V.
self-attention.caption: Self-attention turns each token into a query, a key, and a value (panel B). To re-represent a token, its query is compared by dot product against every token's key, the scores are scaled and softmaxed into weights, and the output is the weighted sum of the value vectors (panels A and C). A token whose key aligns with the query contributes strongly, so each position gathers information from the whole sequence.
self-attention.credit: Shuang Zhang, Rui Fan, Yuti Liu, Shuang Chen, Qiao Liu, Wanwen Zeng

self-consistency.alt: Two rows. The top, greedy decoding, runs one chain of thought to one answer. The bottom, self-consistency, samples several different reasoning chains for the same question that reach various answers, then takes a majority vote to pick the most consistent final answer.
self-consistency.caption: Self-consistency replaces a single greedy chain of thought (top) with many sampled chains (bottom), then returns the answer they agree on most by majority vote. The idea is that a correct answer can be reached by several distinct valid routes while errors scatter, so agreement concentrates on the truth. It buys accuracy with extra inference rather than a bigger model.
self-consistency.credit: Xuezhi Wang, Jason Wei, Dale Schuurmans, Quoc Le, Ed Chi, Sharan Narang, Aakanksha Chowdhery, Denny Zhou

self-refinement.alt: A loop diagram. An input goes into a central Model. The model produces feedback on its own output (left arc, labeled use the model to get feedback on its output), then refines its previous output given that feedback (right arc, labeled use the model to refine its output), cycling back to the model.
self-refinement.caption: Self-refinement turns one attempt into an improvement loop run by the same model: it drafts an answer, critiques its own draft, and rewrites it using that critique, repeating for a few rounds. It works because judging a finished draft is easier than writing it, and gains come purely from spending more inference, with no change to the model's weights.

semantic-search.alt: A 2D plot with three vectors drawn from the origin: a green query vector q in the middle, a blue document vector d1 above it, and a red document vector d2 below it. The angle alpha is marked between q and d1, and the larger angle theta is marked between q and d2.
semantic-search.caption: Semantic search embeds the query and every document as vectors in one space and ranks documents by closeness to the query, usually the cosine of the angle between them. Here document d1 sits at a small angle alpha from the query q, so it is more relevant than d2 at the wider angle theta. Because angle, not shared words, decides the ranking, synonyms and paraphrases still match.

semantic-segmentation.alt: A semantic segmentation map where each pixel is colored by its category: a person painted brown, foliage green, and a cup blue, all against a black background.
semantic-segmentation.caption: Semantic segmentation labels every pixel with the category it belongs to, producing a dense color map rather than a box. Here one region is painted as person (brown), another as plant (green), another as cup (blue); all pixels of the same class share one color.

semi-supervised-learning.alt: Schematic of semi-supervised learning: a small set of labeled cat and dog images and a large pool of unlabeled animal images both feed a processing step that sorts the images into a cat category and a dog category.
semi-supervised-learning.caption: Semi-supervised learning combines a few labeled examples with a large pool of unlabeled ones. The handful of labels anchors the categories, and the structure of the unlabeled data (which images cluster together) is used to sort the rest, reaching better accuracy than the scarce labels could achieve on their own.

sequence-to-sequence.alt: An encoder-decoder diagram. On the left, an Encoder box of recurrent cells reads inputs x0 and x1 and passes a final hidden state h-f across to the right. On the right, a Decoder box of recurrent cells produces outputs step by step, with two feedback options labeled Teacher Forcing (feeding the true previous token) and Using Prediction (feeding the model's own previous output).
sequence-to-sequence.caption: A sequence-to-sequence model has two halves: an encoder reads the whole input sequence into a hidden state, and a decoder generates the output one token at a time, feeding each emitted token back in to inform the next. During training the true previous token is fed back (teacher forcing); at inference the model's own previous output is used. This is the encoder-decoder template behind translation and summarization.

sharpness-aware-minimization.alt: A three-dimensional loss surface for a ResNet trained with sharpness-aware minimization, showing a broad, gently sloping minimum at the center.
sharpness-aware-minimization.caption: Sharpness-aware minimization minimizes the worst-case loss in a neighborhood rather than the loss at a single point, so it converges to a wide minimum like this instead of the narrow, steep basin standard gradient descent finds, which tends to generalize better.

simulated-annealing.alt: Animation of a marker searching a one-dimensional rugged landscape of many peaks and valleys, with a temperature value shown at the top that falls over time as the marker settles toward the lowest valley.
simulated-annealing.caption: Simulated annealing treats the objective as a landscape to descend, but it is willing to move uphill: a worse move is accepted with a probability set by the temperature shown at the top. While the temperature is high the search roams freely and can cross barriers out of shallow valleys; as it cools the search grows pickier and settles into a deep minimum, shifting from exploration to exploitation.

singular-value-decomposition.alt: A commuting diagram. A unit disc with two perpendicular arrows is sent by M directly to a tilted ellipse. The same result is reached the long way: V star rotates the disc, Sigma stretches it along the axes into an ellipse with semi-axes sigma 1 and sigma 2, and U rotates that ellipse into place.
singular-value-decomposition.caption: The singular value decomposition factors any matrix as M equal to U times Sigma times V star, splitting its action into a rotation, a pure stretch, and another rotation. The unit disc becomes an ellipse whose semi-axis lengths are the singular values sigma 1 and sigma 2, the amounts the matrix stretches along its principal axes. Keeping only the largest of these gives the best low-rank approximation, the basis of PCA and model compression.

softmax.alt: Flow diagram where a network produces three real-valued scores 35.4, 38.1, and minus 5.0 for the classes cat, dog, and horse, and a softmax block converts them into the probabilities 0.06, 0.94, and 0.00.
softmax.caption: Softmax turns a vector of real-valued scores (logits) into a probability distribution. It exponentiates each score and normalizes, so the outputs are positive and sum to one, and a modestly larger logit (38.1 versus 35.4) wins a disproportionate share of the probability.

spectral-decomposition.alt: The Mona Lisa shown before and after a horizontal shear. A red arrow lying along the horizontal stays pointing the same way in both images, while a blue arrow is tilted by the transformation.
spectral-decomposition.caption: An eigenvector is a direction a linear transformation leaves pointing the same way, only stretching or shrinking it. Under this shear the red arrow keeps its direction (it is an eigenvector) while the blue arrow is rotated. Spectral decomposition rebuilds a symmetric operator from such special directions and their scale factors (eigenvalues), turning a tangled action into independent stretches along perpendicular axes.

spectral-graph-theory.alt: A graph of eight numbered nodes forming two tightly connected groups, 1-2-3-4 and 5-6-7-8, joined to each other only by two weak edges drawn as dashed lines through node 4.
spectral-graph-theory.caption: Spectral graph theory reads a graph's structure from the eigenvectors of its Laplacian. The low-frequency eigenvectors expose well-connected communities and the weak edges between them (here the dashed bridge), giving a principled cut into two groups; this is the basis of spectral clustering and of defining a notion of frequency on a graph.

speculative-decoding.alt: Several rows of a sentence being generated. In each row, green tokens are draft proposals the large model accepted, red struck-through tokens are draft proposals it rejected, and blue tokens are the large model's correction. Each row commits a run of several tokens at once.
speculative-decoding.caption: Speculative decoding lets a small draft model propose several tokens that the large model then verifies in one parallel pass. Green tokens are accepted draft guesses, a red token is a rejected one, and the blue token is the large model's correction. Because one expensive pass ratifies a whole run of tokens, many tokens are produced per large-model step, and a careful acceptance rule keeps the output identical to the large model's own.
speculative-decoding.credit: Yaniv Leviathan, Matan Kalman, Yossi Matias

spiking-neural-network.alt: Schematic of a leaky integrate-and-fire neuron. Pulse inputs charge a capacitor representing the membrane; the membrane potential V rises with each input, and when it crosses a dashed threshold the neuron emits a spike and resets to baseline.
spiking-neural-network.caption: A spiking neuron does not output a value every step; it integrates incoming pulses, raising its membrane potential V, and fires a single spike only when V crosses a threshold, then resets. Because nothing happens between spikes and the timing of each spike carries information, computation is event-driven and time is a real dimension, unlike a standard continuous-valued unit.

state-space-model.alt: Signal-flow block diagram of a linear state space model: input u enters matrix B and feeds a summing junction whose output is the state derivative x-dot; an integrator produces the state x, which loops back through matrix A and is read out through matrix C, with a direct feedthrough D from u, summing to the output y.
state-space-model.caption: A state space model describes a system by a hidden state that evolves over time. The input enters through a matrix B, the integrated state x feeds back through the transition matrix A, and the output y is projected out by a matrix C (with an optional direct path D). Everything the model knows about the past is compressed into that single running state, which is why a sequence model built this way costs the same per step no matter how long the context.

stochastic-gradient-descent.alt: Contour plot of a bowl-shaped objective with a stochastic gradient descent path that descends toward the center and then jitters in a noisy cloud around the minimum.
stochastic-gradient-descent.caption: Stochastic gradient descent uses a noisy gradient from a small random sample each step, so its path descends roughly toward the minimum and then jitters around it rather than settling exactly. That same noise is what lets it escape shallow traps a smooth method would stall in.
stochastic-gradient-descent.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

structured-decoding.alt: A finite state machine for a floating-point number regular expression, with states 0 to 3 and digit and decimal-point transitions. Below it a vocabulary row labeled Logits shows most tokens masked out in black while only the tokens that keep the output valid (a decimal and a digit) are highlighted; sampling one of them moves the machine to the next state, updating which tokens are allowed.
structured-decoding.caption: Structured decoding guarantees the output matches a formal grammar by masking, not by hope. The grammar is compiled to a state machine; at each step only the tokens that keep the output valid are left unmasked in the logits (the highlighted cells), and every other token is set to zero probability. Sampling an allowed token advances the machine and recomputes the mask, so the full output is certain to parse.

superposition.alt: On the left, three feature visualizations of car parts feeding a single car concept in the middle; arrows then fan out to the right into several different polysemantic neurons (each also encoding things like a tiger, a dog, and a lobster), with a note that the car feature is spread across many polysemantic neurons.
superposition.caption: Superposition is a network storing more features than it has neurons by spreading each feature across many neurons as an overlapping direction rather than giving it a dedicated unit. Here a single car feature has no neuron of its own: it is distributed across several polysemantic neurons that also encode unrelated concepts, which is what makes individual neurons hard to read.
superposition.credit: Olah, Cammarata, Schubert, Goh, Petrov, Carter

supervised-fine-tuning.alt: A three-stage vertical workflow. Stage 1, pretraining, turns web text into a pretrained base model via next-token prediction. Stage 2, supervised fine-tuning, trains the base model on prompt-and-demonstration pairs to produce an instruction-tuned model. Stage 3, reinforcement learning from human feedback, uses a reward model and PPO to produce an aligned assistant model.
supervised-fine-tuning.caption: Supervised fine-tuning is the second stage of the standard pipeline, sitting between pretraining and any reinforcement learning. It trains the pretrained base model on curated pairs of an instruction and an ideal response, using the same next-token loss as pretraining, so the model learns by imitation to follow instructions and answer directly. The later reward-model stage then refines behavior from comparisons rather than single gold answers.

supervised-learning.alt: Flowchart of supervised learning: labeled observations are split into a training set and a test set; the training set fits a machine learner that produces a prediction model, which is then scored on the test set to give performance stats.
supervised-learning.caption: Supervised learning starts from observations already paired with correct labels. Those labeled examples are split into a training set, which fits the model, and a held-out test set, which scores it. The learner becomes a prediction model whose accuracy is measured on data it never trained on, the discipline that separates genuine generalization from memorization.

support-vector-machine.alt: Two classes of points separated by a solid line, with two parallel dashed lines marking the margin on either side. The points lying exactly on the dashed lines are circled, and the gap width is labeled two over the norm of w.
support-vector-machine.caption: Among all lines that separate the two classes, a support vector machine picks the one with the widest margin, the gap to the nearest points on each side. Only those closest points, the circled support vectors, determine the boundary; the margin width equals two over the norm of the weight vector, so maximizing it is what gives the model its strong generalization.

swe-bench.alt: A pipeline diagram: a real GitHub issue and a codebase feed a language model, which produces a generated pull request editing several files; a unit-test table then shows tests failing before the patch and passing after it.
swe-bench.caption: Each SWE-bench task pairs a real GitHub issue with the actual repository, and the model must produce a patch. The patch is graded by running the project's hidden tests: the task counts as solved only when tests that failed before the change pass after it, an executable check with no partial credit.
swe-bench.credit: Carlos E. Jimenez, John Yang, and SWE-bench authors

symbolic-ai.alt: A game search tree with five levels that alternate between maximizing and minimizing players; leaf values at the bottom are propagated upward by taking the max or min at each level, selecting the best move at the root.
symbolic-ai.caption: Symbolic AI produces intelligent behavior by searching a space of explicit possibilities. This minimax tree shows it: each level is a player choosing the largest or smallest value, and the scores at the leaves are propagated up the tree to pick the best move at the root. Reasoning here is search and rule-following over symbols, not learning from data.

synthetic-data.alt: A loop diagram of the Self-Instruct pipeline. A task pool seeds step one, instruction generation by a language model; step two identifies whether a task is classification; step three generates input-output instances; step four filters low-quality or too-similar generations, and the survivors are added back into the task pool to be reused.
synthetic-data.caption: Synthetic data is training data a model generates rather than data collected from people. This Self-Instruct loop shows the pattern: from a small seed pool, a language model writes new instructions and input-output instances, low-quality or near-duplicate items are filtered out, and the survivors are added back to the pool. The filtering step is decisive, since training a model on its own unfiltered output can amplify its errors.
synthetic-data.credit: Yizhong Wang, Yeganeh Kordi, Swaroop Mishra, Alisa Liu, Noah A. Smith, Daniel Khashabi, Hannaneh Hajishirzi

tangent-space.alt: A sphere labeled M with a flat plane labeled T_p M resting against it at a point p, holding two vectors u and v drawn in the plane.
tangent-space.caption: The tangent space at a point p of a manifold M is the flat plane that best touches the surface there, written T_p M. Although the manifold itself is curved, this plane is perfectly flat, so velocity vectors like u and v can be added, scaled, and compared with ordinary linear algebra.

task-decomposition.alt: A tree diagram with the top goal Supper at the root, branching into five subtasks (Menu, Preparation, Cooking, Meal, Cleanup), each of which branches further into concrete leaf tasks such as Menu Selected, Soup Prepared, Soup Cooked, Table Set, and Table Cleared.
task-decomposition.caption: Task decomposition breaks one large goal into a tree of smaller, tractable subtasks. The overall objective (Supper) is split into a few mid-level parts, each of which is split again into concrete actions an agent can carry out one at a time, then combine. The tree also exposes the dependencies (the cooking of a dish follows its preparation) that planning needs to sequence the work.

taylor-expansion.alt: The sine curve in black with a sequence of Taylor polynomials of increasing degree overlaid in color; each higher-degree polynomial hugs the sine over a wider interval around the origin before peeling away.
taylor-expansion.caption: A Taylor expansion approximates a function near a point by a polynomial built from its derivatives there. Centered at the origin, the degree-one term is the straight tangent line, and each higher-degree polynomial adds curvature and finer corrections, matching the black sine curve over an ever-wider interval.

technological-singularity.alt: A logarithmic plot of computing power, calculations per second per 1000 dollars, from 1900 projected to 2100, rising steeply past horizontal reference lines marked one insect brain, one mouse brain, one human brain, and all human brains.
technological-singularity.caption: The singularity thesis rests on exponential growth in computing: on this log plot the calculations a fixed budget buys climb steadily past the capacity of an insect brain, a mouse brain, and eventually one human brain and all human brains combined. Once machine computation crosses and then races beyond human capacity, the argument goes, AI that improves AI could accelerate change beyond human ability to follow or control.
technological-singularity.credit: Courtesy of Ray Kurzweil and Kurzweil Technologies, Inc.

teleology.alt: A table at the center with four labeled causes around it: material cause (wood), formal cause (design), efficient cause (carpentry), and final cause (dining).
teleology.caption: Aristotle explained a thing by four causes, shown here for a table: its matter (wood), its form (the design), the agent that made it (carpentry), and its end or purpose (dining). Teleology is explanation by that last one, the final cause, the goal for the sake of which something exists or acts, as opposed to only the prior causes that pushed it from behind.

temporal-difference-learning.alt: Two columns of firing-rate traces over time, aligned to a conditioned stimulus (CS) and a reward (R). Before learning, the response spikes at the reward. After learning, the spike moves to the CS. When an expected reward is removed, the trace dips below baseline at the time the reward was due.
temporal-difference-learning.caption: The temporal-difference error is the gap between predicted and actual value, and dopamine neurons track it. Before learning the signal fires at the reward (R); after learning it shifts to the earliest predictor, the conditioned stimulus (CS); and when an expected reward is withheld it dips below baseline. This shifting prediction error is exactly the learning signal TD methods compute.

tensor.alt: Four labeled objects in a row: a single number (Scalar, Tensor Rank 0), a column of numbers (Vector, Rank 1), a grid of numbers (Matrix, Rank 2), and a stack of grids forming a cube of numbers (Rank 3).
tensor.caption: A tensor generalizes a number, a vector, and a matrix to any number of axes. The rank is how many indices you need to reach a single element: a scalar is rank 0, a vector rank 1, a matrix rank 2, and a cube of numbers rank 3, which is why a batch of color images naturally becomes a rank-4 tensor.

test-time-compute.alt: A line chart with generation budget on a logarithmic x-axis and math test accuracy in percent on the y-axis. Several selection methods (majority vote, best-of-N weighted, and reward-model-based search) all rise steeply as the budget grows from a few to hundreds of samples, then flatten.
test-time-compute.caption: Test-time compute spends more computation per query to get better answers. As the generation budget grows from a handful of samples to hundreds (x-axis, log scale), accuracy climbs and then flattens, a smooth scaling curve that turns inference into a dial: spend more thinking for harder questions. The gain comes from sampling and selecting, not from a larger model.
test-time-compute.credit: Charlie Snell, Jaehoon Lee, Kelvin Xu, Aviral Kumar

text-to-speech.alt: A block-diagram pipeline reading left to right: Text enters a Text Analysis block, which produces an utterance composed of words, then a Linguistic Analysis block containing Phasing, Intonation, and Duration, which produces an utterance composed of phonemes, then a Wave Form Generation block, which outputs Speech.
text-to-speech.caption: A text-to-speech system converts writing into an audio waveform in stages. Text analysis works out the words, linguistic analysis adds the prosody (phrasing, intonation, and duration) that makes speech sound natural rather than robotic, and waveform generation synthesizes the final audio. The prosody stage is what carries rhythm and stress, so the same sentence can be spoken as a statement or a question.

the-bitter-lesson.alt: Scatter plot with publication date on the horizontal axis and training computation in petaFLOP on a logarithmic vertical axis, with points for many AI systems rising by roughly twenty orders of magnitude from 1950 to the 2020s.
the-bitter-lesson.caption: The bitter lesson argues that general methods which ride increasing computation tend to beat approaches built on hand-coded human knowledge. The resource those methods exploit is shown here: the compute used to train notable AI systems has grown by about twenty orders of magnitude since 1950 (note the logarithmic axis), and the methods that scale with it have repeatedly won.

token-embedding.alt: A two-dimensional plot with four labeled points. France points to Paris and Germany points to Berlin, and the two arrows are nearly parallel and of similar length.
token-embedding.caption: A token embedding maps each token to a learned vector, placing tokens in a continuous space where meaning becomes geometry. Here the arrow from France to Paris runs nearly parallel to the one from Germany to Berlin, so a relationship like capital-of shows up as a consistent direction. Turning discrete symbols into such vectors is what gives the transformer something continuous to compute over.

top-k.alt: A bar chart of next-token probabilities that decline only gradually, with a red cumulative-probability curve. A top-k cutoff at 6 keeps the six leftmost bars and shades off the rest, with the discarded bars annotated as many probable tokens dropped, causing a lack of word diversity.
top-k.caption: Top-k keeps a fixed number of the most probable tokens, here the top 6, and discards the rest before sampling. Because the count is fixed regardless of the distribution's shape, it misbehaves when the model is genuinely uncertain: many roughly-equal, plausible tokens past the cutoff are thrown away, which can starve the output of variety.

top-p.alt: A bar chart of next-token probabilities, highest on the left and falling to a long flat tail, with a red cumulative-probability curve. A top-p cutoff at 0.9 shades off the right portion, but a long stretch of low-probability tokens is annotated as still leading to incoherence.
top-p.caption: Top-p, or nucleus sampling, keeps the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities add up to p (here 0.9) and samples from that nucleus, so the candidate pool grows when the model is uncertain and shrinks when it is confident. Its weakness is visible here: when one token dominates, a fixed top-p can still admit a long tail of low-probability tokens.

topic-modeling.alt: The plate-notation graphical model of Latent Dirichlet Allocation: nodes alpha, theta, z, w and beta connected by arrows, with z and w enclosed in an inner plate labeled N (words) and theta and z enclosed in an outer plate labeled M (documents). Only the word node w is shaded, marking it as observed.
topic-modeling.caption: Latent Dirichlet Allocation models how documents are generated. Each document has a topic mixture (theta), each word's topic (z) is drawn from that mixture, and the observed word (w, the shaded node) is drawn from that topic's word distribution (beta). The inner plate repeats over the words in a document and the outer plate over documents, and fitting the model infers the hidden topics from the words alone.

topological-data-analysis.alt: A persistence diagram scatter plot of feature birth on the x-axis against feature death on the y-axis, with dimension-0 features as red dots and dimension-1 features as blue triangles, all lying above the diagonal; points far from the diagonal persist longest.
topological-data-analysis.caption: Topological data analysis summarizes the shape of data in a persistence diagram. As a distance threshold grows, topological features appear (birth) and vanish (death); dimension-0 points track connected components and dimension-1 points track loops. Points far above the diagonal live across many scales and signal real structure, while those hugging the diagonal are short-lived noise.

topology.alt: Animation of a coffee mug smoothly deforming into a doughnut-shaped torus and back, with the mug's handle becoming the hole of the torus.
topology.caption: Topology studies the properties of a shape that survive continuous stretching and bending, with no tearing or gluing. By that standard a coffee mug and a doughnut are the same object: each has exactly one hole, and the animation deforms one into the other continuously, the handle becoming the torus hole.

tpu.alt: A worked example of a 2 by 2 matrix multiply on a systolic array shown across cycles 0 to 6. The two input matrices are skewed in time and streamed into a grid of cells from the top and the left; each cell multiplies and accumulates, so the partial sums (for example B times 3 then plus A times 2) build up in place until each output cell holds its final dot product.
tpu.caption: A TPU is built around a systolic array, the grid of multiply-accumulate cells shown here. The two matrices are skewed and streamed in from the top and the left, and each cell multiplies its incoming pair and adds it to a running total before passing the operands on to its neighbor. Because each value is reused across the grid instead of being re-fetched from memory, the array sustains a very high rate of matrix arithmetic for little memory traffic.

transfer-learning.alt: A decision tree that classifies transfer learning. It first asks whether the source and target tasks are identical, then whether the domains and the available labels match, branching to leaves labeled traditional machine learning, transductive transfer learning, inductive transfer learning, and unsupervised transfer learning, which further split into domain adaptation, multitask learning, and self-taught learning.
transfer-learning.caption: Transfer learning reuses knowledge from one problem to help another, and its varieties are organized by what the source and target share. When the tasks differ but labels exist for the target, the setting is inductive transfer learning (including multitask learning); when only the domain differs, it is transductive (domain adaptation); when no labels are available, it is unsupervised transfer learning. The tree maps how each case is named.

transformer.alt: Schematic of the full Transformer architecture showing the encoder and decoder stacks with attention and feedforward blocks
transformer.caption: The Transformer replaces recurrence with attention: stacked encoder and decoder blocks of attention and feedforward layers process an entire sequence in parallel.

tree-of-thoughts.alt: Four reasoning topologies. Input-Output goes straight from input to output. Chain of Thought is a single line of boxes. Self-Consistency with CoT runs several parallel chains into a majority vote. Tree of Thoughts is a branching tree where some branches are kept and explored (green) and others are pruned (red).
tree-of-thoughts.caption: Tree of thoughts (right) generalizes a single chain of thought into a search over a branching tree: at each step several candidate next thoughts are proposed, scored, and either expanded (green) or pruned (red), and a dead end can be abandoned to backtrack. This explicit exploration, contrasted here with the linear chain and the parallel vote of self-consistency, helps most on problems that need planning or trying several approaches.
tree-of-thoughts.credit: Shunyu Yao, Dian Yu, Jeffrey Zhao, Izhak Shafran, Thomas L. Griffiths, Yuan Cao, Karthik Narasimhan

turing-test.alt: Diagram of the Turing test showing a human interrogator communicating with a human and a machine through a terminal
turing-test.caption: In the Turing test an interrogator chats with an unseen human and machine and must tell which is which; indistinguishable replies are taken as a pass.

u-net.alt: U-Net architecture drawn as a U: a contracting left path of down-convolutions and max pooling, a bottom, and an expanding right path of up-sampling and up-convolutions, with horizontal concat arrows copying feature maps across at matching resolutions.
u-net.caption: A U-Net is an encoder-decoder shaped like the letter U: the left path downsamples the image into abstract features, the right path upsamples back to full resolution. The horizontal concat connections are the key trick, copying high-detail feature maps across at each level so the decoder can place sharp boundaries that downsampling alone would blur.

underfitting.alt: Three panels fitting polynomials of degree 1, 4, and 15 to the same noisy samples of a curved true function. Degree 1 is a straight line that misses the curve, degree 4 tracks the true function closely, and degree 15 wiggles through every point.
underfitting.caption: The degree-1 model on the left underfits: a straight line is too simple to capture the curve in the data, so it is systematically wrong even on the training samples and its error stays high. Adding capacity (the degree-4 fit in the middle) closes that gap before too much capacity tips over into overfitting on the right.

value-function.alt: Ten small 4-by-4 grids of the FrozenLake task, labeled Step 1 through Step 10. Each cell is shaded by its estimated value (lighter is higher) and carries a red arrow for the greedy action. Cells are marked S (start), F (frozen), H (hole), and G (goal). Over successive steps the shading spreads outward from the goal and the arrows line up into a path.
value-function.caption: A value function assigns every state a number: the expected long-run reward from there. Here value iteration fills in those values over the FrozenLake grid (lighter cells are worth more), and the value spreads outward from the goal G across the steps. Acting greedily, always stepping toward the higher-valued neighbor (red arrows), turns the value function into a policy.
value-function.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.

vanishing-gradient.alt: Plot from minus ten to ten of the sigmoid function as a solid blue S-curve rising from zero to one, and its derivative as a red dashed curve that peaks at about 0.25 at the center and falls to near zero on both sides.
vanishing-gradient.caption: The sigmoid (blue) saturates for large positive or negative inputs, where its derivative (red) is nearly zero, and even at its center the derivative tops out around 0.25. Backpropagation multiplies one such factor per layer, so a chain of these small numbers shrinks the gradient toward zero in early layers, the vanishing gradient that stalls deep training.

variance.alt: A normal curve shaded into one, two, and three standard deviation bands around the mean, labeled with the percentage of area in each band.
variance.caption: The standard deviation, the square root of the variance, is the natural ruler of spread around the mean. About 68 percent of the mass falls within one standard deviation, about 95 percent within two, and about 99.7 percent within three.

variational-autoencoder.alt: Schematic of a variational autoencoder showing the encoder, the sampled latent distribution, and the decoder
variational-autoencoder.caption: A VAE encodes each input as a probability distribution, samples a latent point, and decodes it, so the latent space stays smooth and can generate new samples.

variational-inference.alt: Three plots labeled Step 1, Step 2, and Step K, with curved arrows cycling between them. In each plot a solid blue curve is the true posterior over one variable and a dashed curve is the approximating factor being fitted to it, with the update formula for that factor written below.
variational-inference.caption: Variational inference replaces intractable inference with optimization: it picks a simple, factorized family and fits it to the true posterior. Each plot shows the true posterior over one variable (solid blue) and the approximating factor (dashed) being tuned to match it, updated one coordinate at a time around the cycle. The fit tightens as the bound on the evidence is maximized.

vc-dimension.alt: Four panels showing colored squares in a plane split by straight lines. The top three panels each show three squares that a single line separates into red and blue groups under a different labeling. The bottom panel shows a four-point arrangement that a single straight line cannot separate into the two colors.
vc-dimension.caption: The VC dimension of a classifier is the largest number of points it can label in every possible way. A straight line in the plane can separate any three points under all of their labelings (top three panels), but there exist four-point arrangements no single line can split (bottom panel). So the VC dimension of a line is three, a count of capacity rather than of parameters.

vector.alt: A 3D Cartesian coordinate system with axes x, y, and z. A point in space is located by dropping to its x, y, and z coordinates, and the unit vectors i, j, and k mark the three coordinate directions at that point.
vector.caption: A vector is an ordered list of numbers that names a point, or an arrow from the origin to it. Its coordinates are the amounts along each independent direction (here the unit vectors i, j, and k), so the same rules extend from this 3D picture to the thousand-dimensional vectors that encode data in machine learning.

vector-database.alt: A plane divided into ten colored polygonal cells, each containing one black seed point. Every cell is the region of the plane closer to its own seed than to any other seed, and cell borders fall exactly midway between neighboring seeds.
vector-database.caption: A vector database answers one question fast: which stored vectors are nearest a query. One way to make that fast is to carve the space into regions, one per stored point, so that every location belongs to its closest point (a Voronoi partition). A query then only has to probe the cells near it rather than scan everything, which is the partition-and-probe idea behind index structures for similarity search.

vector-space.alt: Two solid red arrows, v1 and v2, joined tip to tail, with a dashed red arrow v drawn from the start of v1 to the tip of v2 as their sum.
vector-space.caption: A vector space is any set whose elements can be added and scaled while staying inside the set. Addition is the tip-to-tail rule shown here, where v1 and v2 combine into v; together with scaling a vector by a number, these two operations, obeying a short list of axioms, are the entire defining structure that all of linear algebra is built on.

vision-language-model.alt: Vision-language model schematic: an image goes through a vision encoder and a projection module into tokens, a text question goes through an LLM tokenizer into tokens, the two token streams merge into the LLM transformer blocks, which output a text answer.
vision-language-model.caption: A vision-language model gives a language model eyes. A vision encoder turns the image into feature vectors, a projection module maps them into the language model's token space, and these visual tokens join the text tokens in the same transformer, which reasons over both and answers in words (here, that the people are skiing).

vision-transformer.alt: Vision transformer pipeline: an image is cut into a grid of patches, each patch is linearly projected into a patch embedding, a CLS token is prepended, a positional encoding is added, the sequence goes through a transformer encoder, and an MLP head outputs logits.
vision-transformer.caption: A vision transformer treats an image like a sentence. It cuts the picture into fixed-size patches, projects each into an embedding, adds a positional encoding to restore where each patch sat, and feeds the sequence (with a prepended CLS token) through a standard transformer encoder, reading the class from the CLS output. There is no convolution.

weight-decay.alt: Plot of ridge-regression coefficient values against the regularization strength alpha on a log scale; as alpha grows the many coefficient curves all shrink and converge toward zero.
weight-decay.caption: Weight decay adds an L2 penalty on the size of the weights, pulling them toward zero. As the regularization strength rises (moving toward larger alpha), every coefficient shrinks toward zero, trading a little fit for the smaller, simpler weights that tend to generalize better.

weisfeiler-leman-test.alt: Two different graphs drawn one above the other, both colored by the same color-refinement procedure; the red, green, and blue color classes occur in identical counts in both graphs even though the graphs are not isomorphic.
weisfeiler-leman-test.caption: The Weisfeiler-Leman test colors nodes by iteratively hashing each node's color together with its neighbors' colors. These two graphs are not isomorphic, yet they refine to the same histogram of colors, so the test cannot tell them apart. Because standard message-passing networks are exactly as powerful as this test, they cannot distinguish such graphs either, which sets a hard ceiling on their expressiveness.

word-embedding.alt: A two-dimensional plot with four labeled points: France with an arrow pointing to Paris, and Germany with a parallel arrow pointing to Berlin, the two arrows nearly equal in length and direction.
word-embedding.caption: A word embedding places each word at a point in a continuous space, learned so that relationships become geometry. Here the arrow from a country to its capital is almost the same for France to Paris and Germany to Berlin, so the country-to-capital relation is a single shared direction. This is why simple vector arithmetic on embeddings can answer analogies.

word2vec.alt: Two small network diagrams side by side. On the left, CBOW, four context word slots w(t-2), w(t-1), w(t+1), w(t+2) feed arrows into a central target word w(t). On the right, Skipgram, a central word w(t) sends arrows out to the same four context slots. Below each is its objective function.
word2vec.caption: word2vec learns word vectors from a prediction task in one of two directions. CBOW (left) predicts a target word from its surrounding context, while Skip-gram (right) predicts the surrounding context from the target word. The network is never the goal: the weights it learns become the word embeddings, so the proxy task forces useful structure into the vectors.

working-memory.alt: Diagram of the Baddeley and Hitch working memory model. A Central Executive at the top connects with two-way arrows to three subsystems: the Phonological Loop, the Visuo-spatial Sketchpad, and the Episodic Buffer.
working-memory.caption: The influential Baddeley and Hitch model treats working memory as several components, not one store. A central executive directs attention and coordinates a phonological loop for verbal material, a visuo-spatial sketchpad for imagery, and an episodic buffer that binds them. Because verbal and spatial material use different subsystems, two verbal tasks interfere far more than a verbal and a spatial one.

world-model.alt: Diagram of an agent with a world model across three time steps: at each step an observation from the environment is compressed by a Vision model V into a low-dimensional latent vector, a Memory RNN M integrates the history of those codes to predict future states, and a small Controller C uses the representations from V and M to select an action that feeds back into the environment.
world-model.caption: A world model splits an agent into a perception part and a dynamics part. A Vision model (V) compresses each raw observation into a compact latent vector, a Memory model (M) integrates the history of those codes to predict how the latent state evolves, and a small Controller (C) chooses actions from V and M. Once the agent can predict what happens next, it can plan inside its own learned simulation rather than only by acting in the real environment.

zero-shot-learning.alt: Three prompting setups feeding a Transformer decoder marked no parameter update. The zero-shot panel gives only a task description (translate English to French) and the prompt, with no worked examples, and still produces a translation, in contrast to the one-shot and few-shot panels that add examples.
zero-shot-learning.caption: Zero-shot learning asks the model to perform a task from an instruction alone (the zero-shot panel), with no worked examples in the prompt. It relies entirely on knowledge absorbed during pretraining and instruction tuning. The one-shot and few-shot panels show the contrast, where one or several examples are added to the same prompt.
zero-shot-learning.credit: Zhang, Aston and Lipton, Zachary C. and Li, Mu and Smola, Alexander J.
